sections.title: Capacity to Contract
sections.content:
**Lack of capacity:**

• **Minors** - Voidable by minor
• **Mental incapacity** - Voidable if other party knew
• **Intoxication** - Voidable if other party knew

**Voidable = Valid until disaffirmed**

**Minors liable for NECESSITIES (food, shelter, medical)**

sections.title: Defenses to Enforcement
rows: Can be disaffirmed by protected party

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Contract Law: More Than Just Handshakes!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk contracts. Forget the dusty textbooks for a sec. In the real world, contract law isn't just about memorizing definitions; it's about protecting your clients (and yourself!). Think about it: every time a business buys supplies, leases office space, or hires a consultant, there's a contract lurking somewhere. Mess up the formation, and you're looking at a potential legal nightmare. So, nail down these basics – offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality. It's the foundation for so much of what we do!

sections.title: Spotting Illusory Promises: Don't Get Fooled!
sections.content: One HUGE mistake students make is mistaking an illusory promise for valid consideration. An illusory promise sounds like an agreement, but one party can back out whenever they want. For example, 'I'll buy your car if I feel like it' isn't consideration because there's no actual obligation. A valid contract NEEDS a bargained-for exchange. The key difference is that with valid consideration, both parties are bound to do something or refrain from doing something. If one party has an 'out' at any time, it's likely illusory.

sections.title: Sarah's Startup Snafu: The Capacity Catastrophe
sections.content: Sarah, fresh out of college, started a trendy online boutique. She signed a lease for a storefront, but she was only 17 at the time. Later, realizing she couldn't afford the rent, she tried to get out of the lease. Because Sarah lacked the legal capacity (being a minor) to enter into a contract at the time of signing, the lease is voidable at her option. This highlights the importance of verifying the other party's capacity before entering into any agreement! Always consider age, mental competence, and authority to act on behalf of a company.

sections.title: Contract Review: Your CPA Superpower!
sections.content: As a CPA, you'll frequently be asked to review contracts, especially during business valuations or due diligence. Knowing the elements of a valid contract allows you to quickly identify potential red flags. For instance, you might spot a missing 'consideration' clause in a purchase agreement, alerting your client to a significant risk. Or, you might discover that a key supplier contract was signed by someone without the authority to bind the company. Mastering these concepts now will save your clients (and you) from costly legal battles down the road. It is essential to verify all elements are present before moving forward.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Valid contract requires: Offer, Acceptance, Consideration, Capacity, Legality
Offer terminates on revocation, rejection, counteroffer, or lapse
Acceptance must mirror offer (common law)
Past performance is NOT valid consideration
Statute of Frauds: MY LEGS must be in writing
Minors' contracts are voidable (except necessities)
Void = Never existed; Voidable = Valid until disaffirmed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following elements is NOT required for the formation of a valid and enforceable contract?
options: Offer
Acceptance
Written Documentation
Consideration
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A valid contract requires offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality. While written documentation can be helpful for proving the existence and terms of a contract, it is not always a required element for contract formation. Some contracts can be oral and still be enforceable, depending on the jurisdiction and subject matter (e.g., the Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about contracts you make every day. Are they always in writing?

reveal.question: List the three requirements for a valid offer.
reveal.answer:
1. Intent: Objective manifestation of willingness to enter into a contract.
2. Definite Terms: The essential terms of the offer must be clear and certain.
3. Communication: The offer must be communicated to the offeree.

knowledgeCheck.question: Ace Advertising publishes a weekly flyer advertising various products at discounted prices. One item listed is a high-definition television for $200, regularly priced at $800. Bob sees the advertisement and goes to Ace Advertising to purchase the television. Ace Advertising refuses to sell Bob the television at the advertised price, stating that they only had a limited quantity and those have already been sold. Is Ace Advertising obligated to sell Bob the television at the advertised price?
options: Yes, because the advertisement constitutes a valid offer which Bob accepted.
Yes, because Ace Advertising is obligated to honor its advertised prices.
No, because advertisements are generally considered invitations to deal, not offers.
No, unless Bob can prove that Ace Advertising intentionally misled him.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Advertisements are generally considered invitations to deal or invitations to negotiate, rather than offers. Therefore, Ace Advertising is not obligated to sell Bob the television at the advertised price. An offer requires a manifestation of intent to be bound, definite terms, and communication to the offeree. Advertisements typically lack the requisite intent to be bound to anyone who 'accepts' the 'offer'.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what would happen if a store *had* to sell to everyone who saw an ad, regardless of stock.

rows.label: Intent
values: Objective manifestation of willingness to be bound.
Discussion or exploration of potential terms; no intent to be immediately bound.

rows.label: Definiteness
values: Contains definite and certain terms.
Terms may be vague or incomplete.

rows.label: Legal Effect
values: Creates the power of acceptance in the offeree.
Does not create the power of acceptance.

title: Contracts: Performance & Remedies
description: Understand contract performance standards and remedies for breach

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: What happens when contracts aren't fully performed? Understanding performance standards, breach, and available remedies is crucial. The exam tests the difference between complete, substantial, and material breach—and what remedies apply!

sections.title: Levels of Performance
rows: Other party must perform, minus damages

sections.title: Substantial Performance
sections.content:
**When performance is substantial:**

• Performing party acted in good faith
• Defects are minor and not willful
• Essential purpose of contract achieved

**Effect:**
• Other party must pay contract price MINUS cost to remedy defects

**Does NOT apply to sale of goods (UCC requires perfect tender)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Performance Levels
sections.content:
**"CSM"** - Complete, Substantial, Material:

**C**omplete = You're done, get paid in full
**S**ubstantial = Minor issues, get paid minus fixes
**M**aterial = Major breach, other party walks away

**The more complete, the more you get!**

sections.title: Anticipatory Breach
sections.content:
**Before performance is due:**

• Party clearly indicates won't perform
• Must be unequivocal repudiation
• Non-breaching party can:
  - Sue immediately for breach
  - Wait for actual breach
  - Treat as material breach

**Can be retracted before other party relies on it**

sections.title: Damages: Compensatory
sections.content:
**Goal: Put non-breaching party in position if contract performed**

**Types:**
• **Expectation damages** - Benefit of the bargain
• **Direct damages** - Naturally flow from breach
• **Incidental damages** - Costs to find substitute
• **Consequential damages** - Special damages if foreseeable

**Must mitigate damages!**

sections.title: Other Damage Types
sections.content:
**Liquidated Damages:**
• Pre-agreed amount in contract
• Valid if: (1) Actual damages hard to estimate, (2) Amount reasonable
• If excessive = Unenforceable penalty

**Nominal Damages:**
• Small amount when breach proven but no actual loss
• Usually $1

**Punitive Damages:**
• Generally NOT available in contract (only tort)

sections.title: Equitable Remedies
rows: Unique goods/real estate; money damages inadequate
Correct written contract to match intent

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Specific Performance
sections.content:
**Specific performance (forcing performance) available when:**

✓ **Real estate** - Each parcel is unique
✓ **Unique goods** - Rare art, antiques
✗ **Personal services** - NEVER (13th Amendment)
✗ **Ordinary goods** - Money damages adequate

**Court won't force someone to work for you!**

sections.title: Duty to Mitigate
sections.content:
**Non-breaching party must minimize damages:**

• Take reasonable steps to reduce loss
• Cannot recover damages that could have been avoided
• Must not "pile up" damages by inaction

**Example:**
• Employee wrongfully fired
• Must seek comparable employment
• Damages reduced by what could have been earned

sections.title: Excuses for Nonperformance
content: **Impossibility** - Performance literally impossible (death, destruction)
**Impracticability** - Extreme difficulty beyond contemplation
**Frustration of Purpose** - Purpose destroyed by unforeseen event
**Mutual Rescission** - Both parties agree to cancel
**Accord and Satisfaction** - New agreement to settle existing dispute
items: Frustration of Purpose (Principal purpose is substantially frustrated)

sections.content: <!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Substantial Performance: Close Enough for Jazz?
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. 'Substantial performance' isn't 'perfect performance.' Think of it like this: you hire a contractor to build a deck. They put up 95% of it perfectly, but one railing post is slightly crooked. Are you gonna stiff them the whole bill? Probably not. You'll deduct the cost to fix the post. That's substantial performance. Just remember, good faith matters. If they intentionally cheaped out, it's a different story! And remember, this ain't the UCC, so don't expect perfect tender. We're talking about services here.

sections.title: Confusing Substantial Performance & Perfect Tender
sections.content: Students often mix up 'substantial performance' (common law contracts) with 'perfect tender' (UCC - sale of goods). The mistake happens because both concepts deal with performance, but the standards are drastically different. Substantial performance means the contract's essence is fulfilled, even with minor defects. Perfect tender, under the UCC, requires exact conformity to the contract terms. Remember, if it's a service (like building a house), think substantial performance. If it's a tangible item (like buying a car), perfect tender rules. Don't let a rogue tomato ruin your grade!

sections.title: Repudiation Rumble: Bob's Broken Promise
sections.content: Bob contracts with Acme Corp to provide consulting services starting in 3 months. One month later, Bob emails Acme, stating, 'I'm not going to be able to do the work.' This is an unequivocal repudiation. Acme can now immediately sue Bob for breach of contract, even though performance isn't due for another two months. They don't have to wait until Bob actually fails to show up. They can also choose to wait and see if Bob changes his mind, but they aren't obligated to.

sections.title: Spotting Repudiation in Real Life
sections.content: As a CPA, you might encounter anticipatory repudiation when advising clients on business deals. Imagine a client who's selling their business. Before closing, they start dismantling key equipment. That could be seen as a clear indication they won't fulfill the sale agreement. You'd advise your client on their options: sue immediately, wait for the breach, or try to renegotiate. Understanding anticipatory repudiation is crucial for protecting your client's interests and avoiding costly legal battles. It's all about identifying the 'unequivocal' intent to breach.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Substantial performance: Minor defects don't excuse payment
Material breach: Major failure discharges other party
Anticipatory breach: Can sue before performance due
Compensatory damages: Put in position as if performed
Specific performance: Only for unique goods/real estate
No specific performance for personal services
Non-breaching party must mitigate damages

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Builder Bob contracted with Owner Ollie to construct a storage shed for $10,000. Bob completed the shed, but used slightly thinner gauge metal than specified in the contract, resulting in a cost of $500 to replace the metal with the specified gauge. Bob acted in good faith and the shed is fully functional. Which of the following is correct?
options: Ollie owes Bob nothing because Bob did not perfectly perform the contract.
Ollie owes Bob $10,000 because Bob substantially performed the contract.
Ollie owes Bob $9,500 because Bob substantially performed the contract, but Ollie can deduct the cost to remedy the defect.
Ollie can sue Bob for breach of contract and recover damages for the inconvenience.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Substantial performance applies here because Bob acted in good faith, the defect is minor, and the essential purpose of the contract (a functional shed) was achieved. Ollie must pay the contract price minus the cost to remedy the defect. The UCC's perfect tender rule does not apply as this is a construction contract, not a sale of goods.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the elements of substantial performance: good faith, minor defects, and essential purpose achieved.

reveal.question: List three actions a non-breaching party can take when faced with an anticipatory breach.
reveal.answer:
The non-breaching party can:
1. Sue immediately for breach.
2. Wait for actual breach.
3. Treat the anticipatory breach as a material breach and discharge their own obligations under the contract.

rows.label: Applicability
values: Generally applies to service contracts (e.g., construction).
Applies to contracts for the sale of goods (UCC).

rows.label: Performance Standard
values: Requires good faith effort and minor defects are permissible if the essential purpose is achieved.
Requires exact conformity to the contract terms. Any deviation is a breach.

rows.label: Remedy
values: Non-breaching party must pay the contract price less the cost to remedy any defects.
Buyer can reject the goods, accept the goods, or accept part and reject part (subject to certain limitations).

title: Contracts: Third-Party Rights
description: Learn how contract rights can be transferred to third parties

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Contracts don't just affect the original parties! Assignment, delegation, and third-party beneficiaries extend contract rights and obligations to others. Understanding these concepts is essential for REG!

sections.title: Assignment of Rights
sections.content:
**Transfer of contract rights to third party:**

• **Assignor:** Original party transferring rights
• **Assignee:** Third party receiving rights
• **Obligor:** Party who must now perform to assignee

**No consideration required!**

**Generally assignable** unless contract prohibits or would materially change obligor's duty

sections.title: Rights that Cannot Be Assigned
content: Personal service contracts (unique skills)
Rights that would materially increase obligor's risk
Contracts expressly prohibiting assignment
Some wage assignments (limited by law)
Future rights not yet earned
Rights coupled with personal trust (attorney-client)
items: Assignment that significantly changes obligor's risk/duty (e.g., insurance)
Assignment of highly personal services (e.g., paint a portrait)
Assignment prohibited by contract (except right to money/damages)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Assignment vs Delegation
sections.content:
**"Rights are ASSIGNED, Duties are DELEGATED"**

**Assignment:** "You get my right to receive $"
**Delegation:** "You do my job for me"

**Key difference:**
• Assignment transfers BENEFITS
• Delegation transfers BURDENS

sections.title: Delegation of Duties
sections.content:
**Transfer of contract duties to third party:**

• **Delegator:** Original party delegating duty
• **Delegatee:** Third party performing

**Generally delegable** unless:
• Personal skill/judgment required
• Contract prohibits delegation
• Would materially change performance

**Delegator remains liable unless novation!**

sections.title: Novation
sections.content:
**Substitution of parties:**

• New party completely replaces original party
• Original party released from liability
• Requires consent of ALL parties

**Novation ≠ Assignment**
• Assignment: Assignor still secondarily liable
• Novation: Original party completely released

sections.title: Intended vs Incidental Beneficiary
sections.content:
**Intended beneficiary:**
• Parties INTENDED to benefit this person
• Named or identifiable in contract
• Has ENFORCEABLE rights

**Incidental beneficiary:**
• Benefits by chance from contract
• NOT intended by parties
• NO enforceable rights

**Example:** City contracts for road repair → Businesses benefit = Incidental only

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: When Rights Vest
sections.content:
**Third-party beneficiary rights VEST when:**

• Beneficiary learns of contract and assents
• Beneficiary materially changes position
• Beneficiary sues to enforce

**Before vesting:** Original parties can modify/cancel
**After vesting:** Cannot modify without beneficiary's consent

**Timing matters!**

sections.title: Assignment Notice
sections.content:
**Notice to obligor:**

• Not required for valid assignment
• BUT strongly recommended!

**Without notice:**
• Obligor who pays assignor is discharged
• Assignee must sue assignor

**With notice:**
• Obligor must pay assignee
• Payment to assignor doesn't discharge
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Assignment vs. Delegation: Don't Get Bamboozled!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk assignments and delegations. This stuff seems dry, but it's crucial. Think of it like this: assigning a contract is like selling your concert tickets – someone else gets your rights. Delegating is like hiring a caterer for your party – you're still responsible, but someone else is doing the work. The big difference is that with an assignment, you're out of the picture, but with delegation, you're still on the hook! Get this wrong, and you'll be singing the blues on exam day. No consideration needed for assignment? Believe it!

sections.title: Assuming All Contracts Are Assignable
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming all contracts are freely assignable. Students often forget to check for anti-assignment clauses. Just because there's no mention of assignment doesn't mean it's automatically allowed! Also, many overlook the 'materially alters the obligor's duty' exception. If assigning the contract significantly increases the burden on the obligor, it's likely not assignable. Always read the fine print and consider the impact on all parties involved. Don't blindly assume. Err on the side of caution!

sections.title: Sarah's Slippery Assignment Situation
sections.content: Sarah, a recent accounting grad, signed a contract for landscaping services for her new home. The contract explicitly stated 'no assignment without written consent.' Sarah then decided to sell her home and wanted to assign the landscaping contract to the new homeowner. However, the landscaping company refused to consent. Legally, the landscaping company is within its rights. Sarah cannot force the assignment because the contract clearly prohibited it. Always check those assignment clauses!

sections.title: Due Diligence: Know Your Contract Assignments
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter assignment and delegation issues frequently during due diligence for mergers and acquisitions. You need to carefully review all material contracts to determine if they can be assigned to the acquiring company. A contract that's critical to the target company's operations but cannot be assigned without consent can significantly impact the deal's value. Identifying these restrictions early is crucial for negotiating deal terms and avoiding costly surprises later. This applies to real estate leases, supplier contracts, and even employment agreements.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Assignment transfers rights; Delegation transfers duties
Most rights assignable unless personal or prohibited
Delegator remains liable unless novation occurs
Novation requires ALL parties' consent
Intended beneficiaries can sue; Incidental cannot
Beneficiary rights vest once they rely or assent
Notice to obligor is important but not required for validity

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Ollie enters into a contract with Vance, where Ollie will paint Vance's house for $5,000. Ollie, needing immediate funds, assigns his right to receive payment to Wendy. Vance is notified of the assignment. Later, Vance pays Ollie the $5,000. Which of the following is correct?
options: Wendy can recover the $5,000 from Vance.
Wendy can only recover the $5,000 from Ollie.
Vance is not liable to Wendy because he paid the original party, Ollie.
The assignment is invalid because Ollie received consideration from Vance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Once Vance was notified of the assignment, he was obligated to pay Wendy, the assignee. Paying Ollie does not discharge Vance's obligation to Wendy. Wendy can recover from Vance. Ollie is also liable to Wendy. Consideration is not required for the assignment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what happens when the obligor is notified of the assignment.

reveal.question: List three circumstances under which duties cannot be delegated.
reveal.answer: 1. Personal skill/judgment is required. 2. The contract prohibits delegation. 3. Delegation would materially change the performance.

rows.label: What is transferred?
values: Contract rights are transferred to a third party.
Contract duties are transferred to a third party.

rows.label: Original Party's Role
values: Assignor transfers rights.
Delegator transfers duties.

rows.label: Third Party's Role
values: Assignee receives rights.
Delegatee performs duties.

rows.label: Original Party's Liability
values: Assignor is typically not liable after assignment unless there are specific guarantees. Vance has to pay Wendy.
Delegator remains liable even after delegation. Ollie is still liable to Vance.

title: Business Structures: Overview
description: Compare the legal characteristics of different business entities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Choosing the right business structure impacts liability, management, taxation, and more! Understanding the legal characteristics of each entity type helps CPAs advise clients and is essential for REG!

sections.title: Sole Proprietorship
sections.content:
**Simplest structure:**

• No formal creation required
• Owner has unlimited personal liability
• All income/losses on Schedule C
• No separate legal existence
• Terminates on owner's death

**Pros:** Simple, low cost, full control
**Cons:** Unlimited liability, limited capital raising

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Entity Characteristics
sections.content:
**"CALM"** for limited liability entities:

**C**orporation
**A**s an LLC
**L**imited Partnership (LPs only)
**M**embers/shareholders protected

**General Partners and Sole Proprietors = UNLIMITED liability**

sections.title: General Partnership
sections.content:
**Two or more persons carrying on business for profit:**

• Created by agreement (can be oral)
• Partners share profits/losses
• ALL partners have unlimited liability
• Joint and several liability for partnership debts
• Pass-through taxation

**Advantage:** Easy formation, flexibility
**Disadvantage:** Liability exposure

sections.title: Limited Partnership (LP)
sections.content:
**Has two classes of partners:**

**General Partners:**
• Manage business
• Unlimited liability

**Limited Partners:**
• Passive investors
• Limited liability (to investment)
• Cannot manage (or lose protection)

**Requires formal filing with state**

sections.title: Limited Liability Company (LLC)
sections.content:
**Best of both worlds:**

• Limited liability for ALL members
• Flexible management (member or manager-managed)
• Pass-through taxation (default)
• Can elect corporate taxation
• Operating agreement governs

**Most popular new business entity!**

**Requires state filing (Articles of Organization)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: LP Participation
sections.content:
**Limited partner participating in management:**

**Old rule:** Lose limited liability protection
**Modern rule (RULPA):** More protected, but some risk remains

**Safe activities for LPs:**
• Voting on major matters
• Being employee/contractor
• Consulting on business

**Danger:** Acting as if you're a general partner

sections.title: Corporation Basics
sections.content:
**Separate legal entity:**

• Shareholders own; Directors manage
• Limited liability for shareholders
• Perpetual existence
• Formal requirements (minutes, meetings)

**C Corp:** Double taxation (corporate + dividend)
**S Corp:** Pass-through (but restrictions apply)

**Requires state filing (Articles of Incorporation)**

sections.title: Choosing an Entity
sections.content:
**Consider:**

• **Liability protection needed?** → LLC, Corp
• **Number of owners?** → S Corp limited to 100 shareholders
• **Tax treatment preference?** → Pass-through vs C Corp
• **Raising capital?** → Corp preferred by investors
• **Simplicity?** → Sole prop, Partnership
• **Formalities tolerable?** → Corp has most
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Sole Proprietorships: Easy to Start, Easy to Sue
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk sole props. It's the business equivalent of hitting snooze – super easy to set up (or not set up!), but can bite you later. I've seen too many people think, 'Hey, I'll just sell some stuff online!' and then BAM, personal assets are on the line when things go south. Seriously, even if it's just a side hustle, think about liability protection. A simple LLC can save you a world of hurt down the road. Don't learn this the hard way, like my buddy who sold 'artisanal' dog biscuits...

sections.title: Partnership Profit/Loss Allocation Confusion
sections.content: Students often assume that partners share profits and losses equally in a partnership. WRONG! While an equal split is possible, the partnership agreement dictates the allocation. If the agreement is silent, then profits are shared equally, but losses are shared in the same manner as profits. For example, a partnership agreement might allocate profits 70/30 but be silent on losses. In that case, losses are also split 70/30. Don't miss this subtle but crucial point – it shows up on the exam ALL the time!

sections.title: Bob's Blunder: Mixing Business & Personal Funds
sections.content: Bob started a sole proprietorship selling handmade furniture. He used his personal bank account for both business and personal expenses, making it nearly impossible to track his business income and expenses accurately. When audit time came, Bob was scrambling to piece everything together, leading to disallowed deductions and a significant tax bill. The lesson? Keep business and personal finances SEPARATE, even for a sole proprietorship. It's cleaner, more professional, and saves you headaches (and money!) later.

sections.title: Entity Selection: Daily Bread and Butter
sections.content: As a CPA, you'll be guiding clients on entity selection constantly. Think about it: someone starting a coffee shop, a tech startup, or even a freelance writing business. Understanding the liability implications, tax treatment, and capital-raising potential of each structure (sole prop, partnership, etc.) is crucial. You'll need to advise them on the best fit for their specific situation and long-term goals. This isn't just theory; it's a core service you'll provide, impacting your clients' success (and your reputation!).

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sole proprietorship: Simple but unlimited liability
General partnership: All partners have unlimited liability
Limited partnership: GPs unlimited, LPs limited (if passive)
LLC: Limited liability + tax flexibility
Corporation: Limited liability + formal requirements
S Corps and LLCs offer pass-through taxation
C Corps face double taxation

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John is the sole proprietor of 'John's Widgets.' The business incurs a significant debt it cannot pay. Which of the following statements is correct regarding John's liability?
options: John has no personal liability for the debt because 'John's Widgets' is a separate legal entity.
John is only liable up to the amount of capital he initially invested in 'John's Widgets'.
John has unlimited personal liability for the debt, meaning his personal assets are at risk.
John's liability is limited to the business assets of 'John's Widgets'.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: As a sole proprietor, John has unlimited personal liability. This means his personal assets (e.g., house, savings) are at risk to satisfy the business debt. The sole proprietorship is not a separate legal entity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental characteristic of a sole proprietorship regarding personal liability.

reveal.question: How are profits and losses from a general partnership taxed?
reveal.answer: General partnerships are subject to pass-through taxation. This means the partnership itself does not pay income tax. Instead, the profits and losses are passed through to the partners, who report their share of the income or loss on their individual income tax returns. Each partner's share is determined by the partnership agreement.

sections.title: Comparison: Sole Proprietorship vs. General Partnership

rows.label: Number of Owners
values: One
Two or More

rows.label: Liability
values: Unlimited personal liability
Unlimited personal liability (joint and several)

rows.label: Formal Creation
values: No formal creation required
Created by agreement (can be oral)

rows.label: Separate Legal Existence
values: No
No

title: Partnerships & LLCs: Legal Aspects
description: Understand partnership and LLC formation, operation, and dissolution

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships and LLCs are incredibly common business structures! Understanding the legal rules—especially partner rights, fiduciary duties, and dissolution—is critical for REG. Don't confuse legal rules (this lesson) with tax rules (covered later)!

sections.title: Partnership Formation
sections.content:
**Association of two or more persons:**

• Carrying on a business
• As co-owners
• For profit

**No formal agreement required!**

**Factors suggesting partnership:**
• Sharing profits (strongest factor)
• Sharing losses
• Sharing management
• Joint ownership of property

sections.title: Partner Rights
content: **Management** - Equal vote regardless of capital contribution
**Profits/Losses** - Share equally (unless agreed otherwise)
**Inspect books** - Full access to partnership records
**Accounting** - Right to formal accounting
**Partnership property** - Use for partnership purposes
**Indemnification** - Reimbursement for proper expenses
items: Right to Management (Equal say unless agreed otherwise)
Right to Share in Profits/Losses (Equal share unless agreed otherwise)
Right to Indemnification for partnership expenses
Property Rights: Interest in partnership (can be assigned)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Partner Duties
sections.content:
**Partners owe "LOC"** fiduciary duties:

**L**oyalty - Put partnership first
**O**bedience - Follow partnership agreement
**C**are - Act as prudent person

**Same as agent duties!** Partners are mutual agents.

sections.title: Partnership Liability
sections.content:
**Joint and several liability:**

• Each partner liable for ALL partnership debts
• Creditor can sue any partner for full amount
• Partner who pays can seek contribution from others

**Incoming partner:** Not liable for pre-existing debts (unless agreed)

**Outgoing partner:** Remains liable for debts incurred while partner (until creditor releases)

sections.title: Partnership Property vs Partner Property
sections.content:
**Partnership property:**
• Owned by partnership entity
• Used for partnership business
• Cannot be attached by personal creditors

**Partner's interest:**
• Right to share of profits/surplus
• Can be attached by personal creditors (charging order)
• Cannot force liquidation (generally)

sections.title: Dissolution vs Winding Up
sections.content:
**Dissolution = Change in partner relationship**
**Winding up = Process of terminating business**

**Causes of dissolution:**
• Express will of partner to withdraw
• Expulsion per agreement
• Bankruptcy of partner
• Death of partner (unless agreed otherwise)
• Court decree

**After dissolution:** Wind up or continue with buyout

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Partnership vs Partner Property
sections.content:
**Personal creditor of partner:**
• Can get "charging order" against partner's interest
• CANNOT seize partnership property
• CANNOT force dissolution (usually)

**Partnership creditor:**
• Can seize partnership property
• Partners personally liable if partnership assets insufficient

**Keep these creditor types straight!**

sections.title: LLC Formation
sections.content:
**Requires state filing:**

• **Articles of Organization** - Filed with state
• **Operating Agreement** - Governs internal affairs

**Members have:**
• Limited liability (like shareholders)
• Flexible management options
• Pass-through taxation (default)

**Operating agreement is key document!**

sections.title: LLC Management
sections.content:
**Two options:**

**Member-Managed:**
• All members participate in management
• Each member has authority to bind LLC
• Default rule

**Manager-Managed:**
• Designated managers run business
• Non-manager members are passive
• Members can hire outside managers
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Partnership Agreements: Get it in Writing!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. The rule says no formal agreement needed for a partnership, but trust me, you WANT one. Imagine two friends starting a coffee shop, 'Beans & Dreams'. One thinks profits should be reinvested, the other wants a yacht. Without a written agreement outlining profit sharing, management roles, and dispute resolution, you're looking at a legal nightmare. It's like building a house without blueprints – eventually, something's gonna crack. Spend the time and money upfront; your future sanity (and bank account) will thank you.

sections.title: Joint & Several: Don't Get Blindsided!
sections.content: Students often underestimate the impact of joint and several liability. They think, 'Okay, I'm only responsible for my share.' Wrong! If your partner skips town with $100,000 in unpaid bills, the creditor can come after YOU for the entire amount. The key is understanding that as a partner, you're essentially vouching for everyone else. Due diligence is CRUCIAL before entering a partnership. Know your partners, understand their financial habits, and get sufficient insurance coverage. Don't let someone else's mistake become your financial burden.

sections.title: Incoming Partner, Old Debts?
sections.content: Sarah is joining an existing partnership, 'Tech Wizards,' specializing in app development. Tech Wizards already has significant debt from a previous marketing campaign. Sarah is excited to contribute her coding expertise and grow the business. However, Sarah needs to understand that while she is NOT personally liable for Tech Wizards' pre-existing debts, her investment in the partnership IS at risk if the partnership assets are used to satisfy those debts. She should negotiate an indemnity agreement to protect her initial capital contribution.

sections.title: LLC Operating Agreements: Daily Life
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter LLC operating agreements constantly. Think about advising a client forming a real estate investment LLC. You'll need to guide them through crucial decisions like: How are profits and losses allocated? What happens if a member wants to leave? What are the capital call requirements? These questions are all addressed in the operating agreement. A well-drafted agreement minimizes disputes, protects members' interests, and ensures the LLC operates smoothly, allowing your client to focus on building their real estate empire.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership can be oral; sharing profits is key factor
Partners share equally in profits/losses unless agreed
Joint and several liability for partnership debts
Partner's personal creditors: Charging order only
Dissolution = Change in relationship; Winding up = Termination
LLC requires Articles of Organization filed with state
LLCs can be member-managed or manager-managed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important factor in determining whether a partnership exists?
options: Joint ownership of property used in the business.
Sharing gross revenues of a business.
Sharing profits of a business.
Having a written partnership agreement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sharing profits is the strongest indicator of a partnership. While other factors can be suggestive, profit sharing carries the most weight. A written agreement is not required for a partnership to exist.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the ultimate goal of a business is.

reveal.question: Describe the type of liability partners have for partnership debts.
reveal.answer: Partners have joint and several liability for partnership debts. This means that each partner is liable for the entire amount of the partnership's debts, and a creditor can pursue any one partner for the full amount.

knowledgeCheck.question: Unless otherwise agreed, what is the liability of an incoming partner for debts incurred by the partnership *before* their admission?
options: The incoming partner has unlimited personal liability for all pre-existing debts.
The incoming partner has no liability for any pre-existing debts.
The incoming partner's liability for pre-existing debts is limited to their capital contribution to the partnership.
The incoming partner is only liable for pre-existing debts if the other partners become insolvent.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An incoming partner is not personally liable for the partnership's debts before they became a partner. However, their capital contribution to the partnership is at risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that a partner is only liable if they were a partner at the time the debt was incurred.

title: Corporations: Formation & Governance
description: Learn the legal requirements for forming and operating corporations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Corporations are the dominant form of large business organization! Understanding incorporation, the roles of shareholders/directors/officers, and corporate formalities is essential for REG and for advising business clients!

sections.title: Corporation Formation
sections.content:
**Steps to incorporate:**

1. **Choose state** - Delaware popular for flexibility
2. **File Articles of Incorporation** - With state
3. **Pay filing fees** - State fees vary
4. **Adopt Bylaws** - Internal operating rules
5. **Hold organizational meeting** - Elect directors, issue stock

**Corporation exists when articles filed (or effective date)**

sections.title: Articles of Incorporation
sections.content:
**Required contents (vary by state):**

• Corporate name (must include Corp., Inc., etc.)
• Registered agent and address
• Number of authorized shares
• Name(s) of incorporator(s)
• Corporate purpose (can be "any lawful purpose")

**Cannot be changed without shareholder approval**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Corporate Structure
sections.content:
**"SOD"** the hierarchy:

**S**hareholders - ELECT directors, approve major changes
**O**fficers - RUN daily operations
**D**irectors - SET policy, HIRE officers

**Shareholders own it → Directors govern it → Officers run it**

sections.title: Role of Shareholders
sections.content:
**Shareholders:**

• OWN the corporation (residual claim)
• ELECT directors (fundamental right)
• Vote on fundamental changes:
  - Mergers and acquisitions
  - Amendments to articles
  - Sale of substantially all assets
  - Dissolution

**Do NOT manage day-to-day operations!**

sections.title: Role of Directors
sections.content:
**Board of Directors:**

• Set policy and major decisions
• Hire and fire officers
• Declare dividends
• Approve major contracts
• Act through board meetings

**Owe fiduciary duties:** Duty of Care, Duty of Loyalty

**Business Judgment Rule:** Protects good-faith decisions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Piercing the Corporate Veil
sections.content:
**Courts can hold shareholders personally liable if:**

• Corporation is "alter ego" of shareholder
• Commingling personal and corporate funds
• Inadequate capitalization
• Failure to observe corporate formalities
• Fraud or injustice

**Maintain separation!** Separate accounts, proper records, adequate capital

sections.title: Shareholder Meetings
sections.content:
**Annual meeting required:**
• Elect directors
• Other business as needed

**Special meetings:**
• Called by board or specified shareholders
• Only matters in notice can be addressed

**Quorum:** Majority of shares entitled to vote (usually)

**Proxy voting:** Shareholders can authorize others to vote

sections.title: Director Meetings
sections.content:
**Regular meetings:**
• No notice required if time set

**Special meetings:**
• Reasonable notice required

**Quorum:** Majority of directors

**Voting:** Majority of quorum approves

**Written consent:** Can act without meeting if unanimous written consent
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Incorporation: Not as Scary as it Sounds
sections.content: Okay, forming a corporation can seem daunting with all the legal jargon. But trust me, it's like following a recipe. Choose your state (Delaware's the cool kid, but your home state might be cheaper). File those Articles, pay the fee (think of it as your corporation's 'birth certificate' fee). Then, get your bylaws in order – these are your company's internal rules of engagement. Finally, hold that organizational meeting! Elect directors, issue stock, and BAM! You're officially a corporation. Don't overthink it; just follow the steps!

sections.title: Authorized vs. Issued Shares: A Crucial Distinction
sections.content: Students often confuse authorized shares with issued shares. Authorized shares are the maximum number of shares a corporation *can* issue, as stated in the Articles of Incorporation. Issued shares are the shares the corporation has actually sold to investors. Thinking they are the same thing can lead to incorrect calculations of ownership percentages and earnings per share. Remember: authorized shares are the *potential*, issued shares are the *reality*.

sections.title: Sarah's Incorporation Headaches
sections.content: Sarah wants to incorporate her new bakery, 'Sweet Surrender.' She forgets to include the word 'Incorporated' or 'Inc.' in the business name on her Articles of Incorporation. The state rejects her filing. The lesson? Pay attention to the name requirements! Failing to include the proper designation can cause delays and extra fees. Double-check everything before submitting!

sections.title: Incorporation: Daily Life of a Tax Pro
sections.content: As a CPA, you'll encounter incorporation issues frequently. Clients will ask about choosing the right state, understanding the implications of S-Corp vs. C-Corp elections (which affects their tax liability), and ensuring their Articles of Incorporation are properly filed. For example, helping a client navigate the nuances of Delaware incorporation for a tech startup versus a local retail shop is a common scenario. Mastering these basics makes you an invaluable advisor.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Corporation formed by filing Articles of Incorporation
Shareholders elect directors and approve major changes
Directors set policy and hire officers
Officers run daily operations
Directors owe fiduciary duties (Care, Loyalty)
Business judgment rule protects good-faith decisions
Piercing the veil if formalities not followed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is NOT typically required during the initial formation of a corporation?
options: Filing Articles of Incorporation with the state.
Adopting Bylaws to govern internal operations.
Electing officers to manage day-to-day operations.
Holding an organizational meeting to elect directors and issue stock.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Electing officers is generally done *after* the initial organizational meeting where directors are elected. The directors then appoint the officers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the sequence of events in forming a corporation.

reveal.question: Name at least three required contents of the Articles of Incorporation.
reveal.answer: The Articles of Incorporation generally require the corporate name, the registered agent and address, the number of authorized shares, the name(s) of the incorporator(s), and the corporate purpose.

rows.label: Primary Role
values: Own the corporation and elect directors
Manage the corporation's business

rows.label: Day-to-Day Operations
values: Do NOT manage day-to-day operations
Responsible for day-to-day operations

rows.label: Voting Rights
values: Vote on fundamental changes and elect directors
Do NOT vote on fundamental changes in the same way shareholders do, but they make decisions regarding the business.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following corporate actions requires shareholder approval?
options: Hiring a new Chief Financial Officer (CFO).
Declaring a quarterly dividend.
Amending the Articles of Incorporation.
Entering into a routine supply contract.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Amendments to the Articles of Incorporation are fundamental changes that require shareholder approval. The other actions are typically within the purview of the board of directors or management.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about actions that fundamentally alter the corporation's structure or existence.

title: Secured Transactions: UCC Article 9
description: Master the rules for security interests in personal property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Lenders want assurance they'll be repaid! Secured transactions under UCC Article 9 allow creditors to take a security interest in property. Understanding attachment, perfection, and priority is critical for REG!

sections.title: What is a Secured Transaction?
sections.content:
**Creditor takes interest in debtor's property:**

• **Secured party:** Creditor with security interest
• **Debtor:** Person who owes obligation
• **Collateral:** Property subject to security interest

**If debtor defaults:** Secured party can repossess collateral

**UCC Article 9** governs secured transactions in personal property

sections.title: Two-Step Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: ATTACHMENT** (creates security interest)
• Rights against debtor

**Step 2: PERFECTION** (gives priority)
• Rights against third parties

**Without attachment:** No security interest
**Without perfection:** Interest valid but may lose to others

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Attachment Requirements
sections.content:
**"VCR"** for attachment:

**V**alue - Creditor gave value
**C**ollateral - Debtor has rights in collateral
**R**ecord - Security agreement (authenticated) OR possession

**All three required! Record the VCR!**

sections.title: Security Agreement
sections.content:
**Must contain:**

• Debtor's authentication (signature)
• Description of collateral
• Intent to create security interest

**Description can be:**
• Specific ("2024 Toyota Camry VIN #...")
• By type ("all inventory")
• "All assets" NOT sufficient for some collateral

**After-acquired property clause:** Can cover future collateral

sections.title: Financing Statement (UCC-1)
sections.content:
**Filed with state (usually Secretary of State):**

**Must contain:**
• Debtor's name (EXACT!)
• Secured party's name
• Collateral description

**Duration:** 5 years (can file continuation)

**Where to file:** State where debtor is located (organization's state of incorporation)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: PMSI (Purchase Money Security Interest)
sections.content:
**PMSI = Creditor financed the collateral purchase**

**Super-priority over prior perfected interests IF:**

**Inventory:** PMSI perfected BEFORE delivery AND notice sent to prior secured parties

**Non-inventory:** PMSI perfected within 20 days of delivery

**Consumer goods:** PMSI automatically perfected (no filing needed)!

sections.title: Priority Rules
sections.content:
**General priority (first in time):**

1. **Perfected > Unperfected** (always)
2. **Between perfected:** First to file or perfect
3. **Between unperfected:** First to attach

**Exceptions:**
• PMSI has super-priority
• Buyers in ordinary course take free of security interests

sections.title: Default and Remedies
sections.content:
**Upon default, secured party can:**

• Take possession (peacefully or by court)
• Dispose of collateral (sale, lease)
• Keep collateral in satisfaction (strict foreclosure)

**Disposition must be commercially reasonable**

**Proceeds:** Apply to debt; surplus to debtor; deficiency owed by debtor
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: UCC Article 9: It's All About the Benjamins
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. UCC Article 9 might seem dry, but it's the backbone of secured lending. Banks aren't just handing out money willy-nilly; they want collateral! Think of it like this: they want to be first in line if the borrower goes belly up. Understanding attachment and perfection is crucial. Mess this up, and your client's loan could be unsecured, making them just another creditor waiting in line. Nobody wants that! So, pay attention, this is where the real money is made (and protected).

sections.title: Attachment vs. Perfection: The Big Mix-Up
sections.content: Students often confuse attachment and perfection. Attachment is about creating the security interest between the debtor and secured party. Perfection is about establishing priority against other creditors. A common mistake is thinking attachment automatically means perfection. It doesn't! You can have attachment without perfection. To avoid this, remember the order: first, attachment (agreement, value given, debtor has rights), then perfection (usually by filing a financing statement). Think of attachment as the agreement, and perfection as telling the world.

sections.title: Bob's Big Blunder: The Unperfected Security Interest
sections.content: Bob, a small business owner, borrowed $50,000 from First National Bank, using his equipment as collateral. The bank filed a financing statement, but they forgot to include Bob's correct legal name (they used his nickname). Later, Bob defaulted, and another creditor, Second State Bank, who *did* perfect their interest properly, claimed the equipment. The result? Second State Bank gets the equipment. Bob's blunder cost First National Bank a lot of money and taught them a valuable lesson about paying attention to detail!

sections.title: Due Diligence: Where UCC Article 9 Lives
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter UCC Article 9 during due diligence for mergers and acquisitions. You'll need to verify that the target company's assets are free and clear of any prior security interests. This involves searching UCC filings to identify any existing liens. Imagine your client is about to acquire a company for $10 million, only to discover a $2 million UCC lien that wasn't disclosed. Understanding UCC Article 9 is critical to protect your client and ensure they're not inheriting unexpected liabilities. It's all about knowing who has the first claim on those assets!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Attachment requires: Value, Collateral rights, Record (VCR)
Perfection gives priority over third parties
Most perfection by filing financing statement (UCC-1)
Debtor's name must be EXACT on financing statement
PMSI gets super-priority (special timing rules)
Consumer goods PMSI: Automatic perfection
Perfected beats unperfected; First to file/perfect wins

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the difference between attachment and perfection in secured transactions under UCC Article 9?
options: Attachment grants the secured party rights against third parties, while perfection grants rights against the debtor.
Attachment requires filing a financing statement, while perfection only requires a security agreement.
Attachment establishes the security interest between the debtor and secured party, while perfection gives the secured party priority over other creditors.
Attachment is optional, while perfection is always required for a valid security interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Attachment creates the security interest between the debtor and secured party. Perfection provides the secured party with priority over other creditors who may have a claim to the same collateral. Without attachment, there is no security interest at all.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who each step is protecting the secured party against.

reveal.question: What are the three essential requirements for a valid security agreement under UCC Article 9?
reveal.answer: 1. Debtor's authentication (signature). 2. Description of the collateral. 3. Intent to create a security interest.

knowledgeCheck.question: Debtor, David, grants Secured Party, Bank, a security interest. Which of the following descriptions of collateral in the security agreement would be considered insufficient under UCC Article 9, thereby potentially invalidating the security interest in the described property?
options: "All equipment located at 123 Main Street, Anytown, USA"
"All inventory"
"2023 Ford F-150, VIN: 12345ABCDEFG67890"
"All assets"
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under UCC Article 9, a description of 'all assets' is generally NOT sufficient for some types of collateral. A more specific description is required to provide adequate notice to third parties. While some jurisdictions may allow 'all assets' for certain types of collateral, it is generally considered insufficient and therefore the best answer here. The other options provide a sufficiently specific description.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what provides the MOST detail and clarity to other potential creditors.

title: Bankruptcy: Chapters 7, 11, 13
description: Understand the major types of bankruptcy proceedings

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Bankruptcy affects creditors, debtors, and businesses alike! Understanding the differences between liquidation (Ch. 7), reorganization (Ch. 11), and individual debt adjustment (Ch. 13) is essential for advising clients and passing REG!

sections.title: Chapter 7: Liquidation
sections.content:
**"Fresh start" through liquidation:**

• Trustee collects and sells non-exempt assets
• Proceeds distributed to creditors by priority
• Remaining debts discharged (with exceptions)

**Means test:** Individuals must qualify (income below median)

**Not available:** If income above median and can pay some debts

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Bankruptcy Chapters
sections.content:
**"7-11-13"** like convenience stores:

**7** = LIQUIDATE (sell everything, quick)
**11** = REORGANIZE (keep business running)
**13** = REPAY over time (wage earner plan)

**7 = Sell | 11 = Save business | 13 = Salary pays**

sections.title: Voluntary vs Involuntary
sections.content:
**Voluntary:** Debtor files petition

**Involuntary (creditors force):**
• Only Chapters 7 and 11
• If 12+ creditors: Need 3 petitioning creditors
• If fewer than 12: One creditor sufficient
• Total claims must be at least ~$18,600
• Debtor not paying debts as they become due

**Cannot file involuntary against:** Farmers, nonprofits

sections.title: Automatic Stay
sections.content:
**Immediate upon filing:**

• ALL collection efforts STOP
• No lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures
• No phone calls demanding payment
• Secured creditors can seek relief from stay

**Powerful protection for debtor!**

**Violation = Contempt of court**

sections.title: Priority of Claims
content: **1. Secured creditors** (from collateral proceeds)
**2. Domestic support obligations** (alimony, child support)
**3. Administrative expenses** (trustee fees, attorney fees)
**4. Gap claims** (involuntary - between filing and order)
**5. Employee wages** (up to ~$15,150, within 180 days)
**6. Employee benefit plan contributions**
**7. Consumer deposits** (up to ~$3,350)
**8. Tax claims** (certain taxes)
**9. General unsecured creditors** (pro rata)

sections.title: Non-Dischargeable Debts
sections.content:
**Cannot be discharged (survive bankruptcy):**

• Student loans (unless undue hardship)
• Alimony and child support
• Most taxes (within 3 years)
• Debts from fraud or false statements
• Debts not listed in petition
• Fines and penalties to government
• Personal injury from DUI

**These must still be paid!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Student Loans
sections.content:
**Student loans are generally NOT dischargeable!**

**Exception:** Prove "undue hardship" (very difficult standard)

**Common misconception:** Filing bankruptcy eliminates student loans → FALSE

**They survive both Chapter 7 AND Chapter 13!**

sections.title: Chapter 11: Reorganization
sections.content:
**Business continues operating:**

• Debtor usually remains in possession ("DIP")
• Files reorganization plan
• Creditors vote on plan
• Court confirms plan
• Business pays debts over time

**Allows business to survive and pay creditors more than liquidation**

sections.title: Chapter 13: Wage Earner Plan
sections.content:
**Individual debt adjustment:**

• Regular income required
• Keep property while repaying
• 3-5 year repayment plan
• Debt limits apply (~$465,000 unsecured)

**Advantages over Ch. 7:**
• Keep property
• More debts may be discharged
• Catch up on mortgage/car payments
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Bankruptcy: Not Just for Big Corporations
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Bankruptcy sounds scary, right? Like something only Enron deals with. But Chapter 7, 11, and 13 are bread and butter for a lot of folks, especially small businesses. Don't think of it as a failure, sometimes it's a necessary reset. I had a client, a local bakery, drowning in debt. Chapter 11 helped them restructure, renegotiate with suppliers, and now they're thriving. Remember, it's a tool, not a tombstone. Know the rules, and you can advise clients on how to use it wisely. Think of it as financial CPR.

sections.title: Chapter Confusion: 7 vs. 11 vs. 13
sections.content: Students often mix up Chapter 7 and Chapter 11. They both involve debt relief, but the key difference is that Chapter 7 is liquidation (assets sold off), while Chapter 11 is reorganization (business continues under a plan). Chapter 13 is for individuals with regular income to create a repayment plan. Don't just memorize the names; understand the *purpose* of each. Ask yourself: is the debtor trying to stay in business (11), get a fresh start by selling assets (7), or repay debts over time (13)? Remembering the goal helps you choose the right chapter.

sections.title: Sarah's Struggle: Involuntary Bankruptcy
sections.content: Sarah ran a small clothing boutique and owed suppliers $60,000. She had 5 creditors, and one, owed $45,000, filed an involuntary Chapter 7 petition against her. Because Sarah had fewer than 12 creditors, only one creditor needed to file. The lesson here is that even small businesses are vulnerable to involuntary bankruptcy if they can't manage their debts, and the rules differ based on the number of creditors involved.

sections.title: Means Test: Real-World Application
sections.content: In practice, you'll use the means test primarily when advising individual clients considering Chapter 7 bankruptcy. You'll need to gather detailed income information and compare it to the state's median income for a household of their size. A common scenario: a client makes $70,000 in a state where the median income is $65,000. Even though they are above the median, you still need to perform the second part of the means test to determine if they have sufficient disposable income to repay their debts. It's not just a simple income cutoff!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Chapter 7: Liquidation, fresh start, means test required
Chapter 11: Business reorganization, debtor stays in control
Chapter 13: Wage earner repayment plan, 3-5 years
Automatic stay stops ALL collection immediately
Priority: Secured → Support → Admin → Wages → Taxes → Unsecured
Student loans, support, and recent taxes NOT dischargeable
Involuntary: Only Ch. 7 and 11, requires creditor petition

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual debtor with income above the state median files for Chapter 7 bankruptcy. After deducting allowable expenses, it is determined that the debtor could pay a significant portion of their unsecured debts over a five-year period. Which of the following is most likely to occur?
options: The debtor will automatically be denied Chapter 7 bankruptcy.
The court will convert the case to Chapter 13 bankruptcy if the debtor consents.
The court will dismiss the Chapter 7 case or convert it to Chapter 13 with the debtor's consent, based on the means test.
The debtor will be required to reaffirm their debts and establish a repayment plan outside of bankruptcy.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The means test is used to determine if a debtor with above-median income can qualify for Chapter 7. If the debtor has sufficient disposable income to repay a significant portion of their debts, the court may dismiss the Chapter 7 case or convert it to Chapter 13, provided the debtor agrees to the conversion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of the means test in Chapter 7 bankruptcy.

reveal.question: What is the minimum number of creditors required to file an involuntary Chapter 7 bankruptcy petition if the debtor has 12 or more creditors?
reveal.answer: Three creditors are required to file an involuntary Chapter 7 bankruptcy petition if the debtor has 12 or more creditors. These creditors must collectively hold at least $18,600 in unsecured claims as of 2023.

rows.label: Initiation
values: Debtor files petition
Creditors file petition

rows.label: Applicable Chapters
values: Chapter 7, 11, 13
Chapter 7 and 11 only

rows.label: Debtor Solvency
values: Debtor's solvency is not a barrier
Debtor must generally not be paying debts as they become due

rows.label: Creditor Requirements
values: None
Specific requirements for number of creditors and amount of claims

title: Secured Transactions: UCC Article 9
description: Master secured transactions including attachment, perfection, and priority

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Secured transactions govern how creditors protect their interests in collateral. Understanding attachment, perfection, and priority is essential for advising business clients and is heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: What is a Security Interest?
sections.content:
**Security interest = Interest in personal property that secures payment**

**Key terms:**
• **Debtor** - Person who owes the debt
• **Secured party** - Creditor with security interest
• **Collateral** - Property securing the debt
• **Security agreement** - Contract creating security interest

sections.title: Attachment: Creating Security Interest
sections.content:
**Three requirements for attachment:**

1. **Value given** - Creditor gives something (loan, credit)
2. **Debtor has rights** - In the collateral
3. **Security agreement** - Authenticated description of collateral

**When all three occur = Security interest ATTACHES**

**Attachment makes it enforceable against debtor**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Attachment - VRS
sections.content:
**"VRS"** for Attachment:

**V**alue given by secured party
**R**ights in collateral (debtor has)
**S**ecurity agreement (authenticated)

**"Very Risky Securities need all three!"**

sections.title: Perfection: Protecting Against Third Parties
sections.content:
**Perfection = Notice to the world of security interest**

**Methods of perfection:**
• **Filing** - UCC-1 financing statement (most common)
• **Possession** - Creditor takes collateral
• **Control** - Bank accounts, investment securities
• **Automatic** - PMSIs in consumer goods

**Perfection protects against other creditors!**

sections.title: Purchase Money Security Interest (PMSI)
sections.content:
**PMSI = Security interest in goods securing their purchase price**

**Example:** Bank loans money to buy equipment, takes security interest in that equipment

**Special priority rules:**
• PMSI in inventory - Must perfect BEFORE delivery
• PMSI in other goods - 20 days after delivery to perfect

**PMSIs get SUPER-PRIORITY over earlier-filed interests!**

sections.title: Priority Rules
rows: First to file or perfect wins
Buyer takes free of security interest

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Buyer in Ordinary Course
sections.content:
**A buyer in ordinary course of business takes FREE of security interest!**

**Example:**
• Bank has security interest in store's inventory
• Customer buys item from store (ordinary course)
• Customer owns item FREE of bank's security interest

**Buyer must not know interest is unauthorized**

sections.title: Default and Remedies
sections.content:
**Upon default, secured party may:**

• Take possession of collateral (self-help if no breach of peace)
• Sell collateral (commercially reasonable manner)
• Accept collateral in satisfaction (strict foreclosure)
• Sue on the debt

**Surplus goes to debtor; deficiency owed by debtor**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: UCC Article 9: It's All About Who Gets Paid First
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. UCC Article 9 can feel like alphabet soup. But boil it down: it's about who gets dibs on the assets when a borrower goes belly up. Think of it like a pie-eating contest where everyone's fighting for the last slice. The secured party with the perfected security interest gets the biggest piece, maybe even the whole pie! So, pay attention, because understanding this stuff can save your client (and your career) a whole lot of heartache down the road. It's not just theory; it's real-world money at stake.

sections.title: Attachment vs. Perfection: Don't Mix 'Em Up!
sections.content: Students often confuse attachment and perfection. Attachment means the security interest is enforceable against the debtor. Perfection, on the other hand, is about making that interest enforceable against other creditors. Think of attachment as having a key to the house, and perfection as posting a 'No Trespassing' sign visible to everyone else. You can't perfect without attachment, but you can have attachment without perfection. Failing to perfect properly can leave your client in line behind other creditors, meaning they might not get paid if the debtor defaults!

sections.title: Sarah's Inventory Nightmare: A Priority Problem
sections.content: Sarah's startup, 'Gadgets Galore,' obtained a $50,000 loan from First Bank, secured by its inventory. First Bank filed a financing statement. Later, Sarah got another $30,000 loan from Second Bank, also secured by inventory, and Second Bank notified First Bank of its purchase money security interest (PMSI). Gadgets Galore defaulted. Because Second Bank properly notified First Bank and has a PMSI in inventory, it has priority over First Bank's security interest in that inventory, even though First Bank filed first. Understanding PMSI rules is crucial for determining priority!

sections.title: Due Diligence: UCC Searches are Your Best Friend
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter secured transactions when performing due diligence for a merger or acquisition. You'll need to conduct UCC searches to identify any existing security interests in the target company's assets. Imagine advising a client to acquire a business only to discover later that a bank has a prior claim on all the equipment. That's a deal-breaker! Mastering UCC Article 9 allows you to identify potential risks and advise your client accordingly, preventing costly mistakes and ensuring a smoother transaction. It's a critical skill for any CPA working in M&A or business valuation.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Attachment: Value + Rights + Security Agreement
Perfection: Filing, possession, control, or automatic
File UCC-1 with Secretary of State (5-year duration)
First to file or perfect generally wins priority
PMSI has super-priority if timely perfected
Buyer in ordinary course takes free of security interest
Upon default: Repossess, sell, apply proceeds

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT required for a security interest to attach?
options: The secured party gives value.
The debtor has rights in the collateral.
A security agreement exists that describes the collateral.
The secured party perfects the security interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Perfection is not required for attachment. Attachment establishes the security interest between the debtor and the secured party. Perfection protects the secured party's interest against third parties.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what creates the initial agreement vs. what protects it from others.

reveal.question: Define 'Perfection' in the context of secured transactions.
reveal.answer: Perfection is the process by which a secured party provides notice to the world of their security interest in the collateral, thereby protecting their interest against other creditors who may also have a claim against the same collateral. It is generally achieved through filing a financing statement, taking possession of the collateral, or control.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Creates security interest enforceable against the debtor.
Protects the secured party's interest against third parties (other creditors).

rows.label: Requirements
values: Value given, debtor has rights in collateral, security agreement.
Attachment AND taking steps to notify third parties (e.g., filing, possession, control).

rows.label: Timing
values: Occurs before or simultaneously with perfection.
Occurs after attachment.

title: Business Entity Formation: Sole Proprietorships
description: Understand the simplest form of business organization

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The sole proprietorship is the most common and simplest business form! Understanding its advantages and limitations helps advise clients choosing an entity structure. REG tests entity selection extensively!

sections.title: What is a Sole Proprietorship?
sections.content:
**One person owns and operates the business**

• No separate legal entity
• Owner IS the business
• No formal filing required to start
• May need licenses/permits

**Default structure when individual starts business alone**

sections.title: Formation Requirements
sections.content:
**Simple formation:**

• Just start doing business!
• May need:
  - Business license (local)
  - DBA/Fictitious name filing
  - Industry-specific permits
  - EIN (if employees)

**No state filing required for the entity itself**

sections.title: Advantages
content: **Easy to form** - No paperwork or legal fees
**Complete control** - Owner makes all decisions
**Simple taxation** - Report on Schedule C
**No double taxation** - Income flows to personal return
**Easy to dissolve** - Just stop doing business
**Minimal compliance** - No annual reports or meetings
items: Easiest and cheapest to form (No filing required)

sections.title: Disadvantages
content: **Unlimited personal liability** - Owner liable for ALL business debts
**Self-employment tax** - On all net earnings (15.3%)
**Limited capital raising** - Cannot sell ownership interests
**Limited life** - Ends when owner dies/stops
**No employee benefits** - Cannot be own employee
items: Unlimited personal liability for business debts
Limited access to capital (loans hard to get)
Business ends upon death of owner
Self-employment tax on all net earnings

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Sole Prop Characteristics
sections.content:
**"SIMPLE but RISKY"**

**SIMPLE:**
• Single owner
• Individual control
• Minimal paperwork
• Personal return (Schedule C)
• Low cost
• Easy start/stop

**RISKY = Unlimited personal liability!**

sections.title: Tax Treatment
sections.content:
**Schedule C (Form 1040):**

• Report income and expenses
• Net profit = Self-employment income
• Subject to:
  - Income tax (regular rates)
  - Self-employment tax (15.3%)
  - Deduct 50% of SE tax above the line

**Quarterly estimated payments typically required**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Liability
sections.content:
**Business debts = Personal debts!**

**Example:** Sole proprietor's business is sued for $500,000

**At risk:**
• Business assets
• Personal home
• Personal bank accounts
• Personal investments
• ALL personal assets!

**No liability protection whatsoever**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Sole Proprietorships: The OG Hustle
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk sole props. Think of it as the lemonade stand of business entities. No fancy paperwork needed to start – just dive in! You're basically the business. Tax-wise, it's all reported on your Schedule C. I've seen folks get tripped up thinking they need to file separate biz returns. Nope! Keep it simple. But don't forget those local licenses and permits. Ignoring those is like forgetting the sugar in your lemonade – nobody wants it!

sections.title: EIN Confusion: Employees vs. No Employees
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking you *always* need an EIN for a sole proprietorship. Not true! You only need one if you have employees or if you're operating as a corporation or partnership (which, by definition, you aren't as a sole prop). Students often assume an EIN is required for *any* business activity. The key difference is whether you're hiring help or just doing your own thing. If you're flying solo, stick with your SSN for tax purposes.

sections.title: Sarah's Sewing Side Hustle
sections.content: Sarah loves sewing and starts selling her creations online. She doesn't file any paperwork beyond getting a business license in her city. A year later, she's audited and claims ignorance about reporting the income. The lesson here is that even though the formation is simple, you're still responsible for reporting all income on Schedule C of your Form 1040. No formal entity doesn't mean no taxes!

sections.title: New Client Intake: Sole Prop Red Flags
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter sole proprietorships all the time, especially with new clients. Red flags to watch for: unreported income on Schedule C, claiming business expenses without proper documentation, and failure to obtain necessary local licenses. Often, they're unaware of their responsibilities, so a gentle but firm explanation of tax obligations and record-keeping requirements is crucial. This upfront education saves headaches (and potential penalties) down the line.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Simplest business form - no state filing required
Owner has complete control but unlimited liability
Income reported on Schedule C (Form 1040)
Subject to self-employment tax (15.3%)
Default structure for individual doing business alone
Cannot raise capital by selling ownership
Business ends when owner dies or stops operating

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions is ALWAYS required to legally establish a sole proprietorship?
options: Filing articles of organization with the state.
Obtaining an Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS.
Obtaining a business license from the local government.
None of the above; no formal filing is required at the state level to establish a sole proprietorship.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sole proprietorships are the simplest form of business to establish. Unlike corporations or LLCs, they do not require formal filing with the state. However, depending on the nature of the business and its location, licenses, permits, or a DBA may be necessary.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the defining characteristic of a sole proprietorship - it's extension of the owner.

reveal.question: Is a sole proprietorship considered a separate legal entity from its owner?
reveal.answer: No, a sole proprietorship is NOT a separate legal entity from its owner. The owner and the business are considered one and the same for legal and tax purposes.

sections.title: Knowledge Check: When is an EIN Required?

knowledgeCheck.question: Under which of the following circumstances would a sole proprietor MOST likely be required to obtain an Employer Identification Number (EIN) from the IRS?
options: When the sole proprietor's business generates more than $10,000 in annual revenue.
When the sole proprietor intends to incorporate the business in the future.
When the sole proprietor hires employees.
When the sole proprietor's business operates under a name different from the owner's legal name (DBA).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A sole proprietor is generally required to obtain an EIN if they hire employees. While a DBA (doing business as) might be used, it doesn't automatically trigger the need for an EIN unless employees are hired. Revenue levels and future incorporation plans do not necessitate an EIN on their own.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about why the IRS assigns EINs.

title: Business Entity Formation: LLCs
description: Master Limited Liability Company formation and characteristics

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: LLCs combine the best features of corporations and partnerships! They're the most popular choice for new businesses. Understanding LLC formation, liability protection, and tax flexibility is essential for REG!

sections.title: What is an LLC?
sections.content:
**Limited Liability Company:**

• Separate legal entity from owners
• Owners called "members"
• Combines liability protection with tax flexibility
• State law entity (not federal)

**Best of both worlds: Corp liability + Partnership taxation**

sections.title: Formation Requirements
