sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Scholarships
sections.content:
**Scholarships are TRICKY for support test:**

**Qualifying Child:**
• Scholarships NOT counted in support
• Student's summer job earnings ARE counted

**Example:** College student with $30,000 scholarship, $5,000 job income
• Total support: $20,000
• Student provides: $5,000 (25%)
• Still QC because student didn't provide >50%

**Scholarships don't count as support from ANYONE!**

sections.title: Tiebreaker Rules
sections.content:
**If multiple taxpayers could claim same child:**

1. **Parent over non-parent**
2. **If both parents:** Parent with whom child lived longer
3. **If equal time:** Parent with higher AGI
4. **If non-parents:** Highest AGI

**Only one taxpayer can claim each dependent!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Dependency: It's All About the Details!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Dependency can be a headache. I've seen seasoned pros trip up on the 'Residency' requirement. It's not just 'lived with you,' it's 'more than half the year!' Think of it like this: if they were living with you more than they were at college, bingo. Less than? No dice. Don't skim the dates. Double-check everything. The IRS is watching, and they love to pounce on these deductions. So, slow down, read carefully, and save yourself the audit nightmare.

sections.title: Income Limits: QR vs. QC Confusion
sections.content: A very common mistake is applying the Qualifying Relative (QR) income test to a Qualifying Child (QC). Remember, there is NO income limit for a QC! Students often get tripped up because they're so used to thinking about income limits in the context of dependents. If the person meets all the 'CARES' test criteria, their income is irrelevant. Only when the 'CARES' test fails and you're considering the QR test does the income limit come into play. Focus on the QC rules first, and only worry about QR if QC fails.

sections.title: Sarah and Her Student Son: A Dependency Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah supports her 23-year-old son, David, who is a full-time student. David earned $6,000 from a summer internship. He lived with Sarah for 8 months of the year. Can Sarah claim David as a dependent? The key here is to apply the 'CARES' test. David meets the close relative, age (under 24 and a student), and residency tests. Since there is no income test for a QC, and there is no indication David filed a joint return, Sarah can claim David as a dependent, even with his $6,000 income.

sections.title: Client Intake: Digging for Dependency Details
sections.content: When interviewing new clients, especially those with young adult children, probe for details beyond surface-level answers. Don't just ask, 'Did your child live with you?' Ask, 'How many nights did they spend at your home versus at college, and for what periods?' Also, always ask about income sources, even if seemingly small. That part-time job or summer internship could push them over the QR income limit, or affect the 'Eliminate Joint Return' requirement. Good client intake saves you time and protects your client (and you!) from penalties.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Qualifying Child: CARES (Close, Age, Residency, Eliminate joint return, Support)
QC age: Under 19 (or 24 if student, any if disabled)
QC support: Child can't provide MORE than half own support
Qualifying Relative: Support, Income, Not QC, Related/Household
QR income: Must be below $5,050 (2024)
QR support: YOU must provide MORE than half
Scholarships: Not counted for support test

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John, age 22, is supported by his parents and attends college full-time. He earned $10,000 during the year working part-time. He lived at home with his parents for 8 months of the year. Which of the following is TRUE regarding whether John qualifies as his parents' qualifying child?
options: John can be claimed as a qualifying child because he is under age 24 and a full-time student.
John cannot be claimed as a qualifying child because his income exceeds the threshold for a qualifying relative.
John can be claimed as a qualifying child if he did not provide more than half of his own support, even though he earned $10,000.
John cannot be claimed as a qualifying child because he did not live with his parents for the entire year.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The income test only applies to qualifying relatives, not qualifying children. The relevant tests for a qualifying child here are age, residency, and support. John meets the age test (under 24 and a student) and residency test (lived with parents more than half the year). The question implies the parents provided more than half of John's support, so he can be a qualifying child.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the CARES test applies to qualifying children. Focus on which elements of CARES are relevant in this scenario.

reveal.question: List the elements of the CARES test for determining if someone is a Qualifying Child.
reveal.answer:
**C**lose relative (child, sibling, descendant)
**A**ge (under 19, or under 24 if student, or any age if disabled)
**R**esidency (lived with you more than half year)
**E**liminate joint return (child can't file MFJ with spouse)
**S**upport (child didn't provide more than half of their own support)

rows.label: Income Limit
values: None
Dependent's gross income must be less than $4,700 (2024 amount, subject to change).

rows.label: Relationship
values: Child, sibling, descendant
Many relatives qualify, or unrelated individual living entire year in taxpayer's home

rows.label: Support Test
values: Dependent didn't provide more than half of their own support
Taxpayer provided more than half of dependent's total support

rows.label: Age Test
values: Under 19, or under 24 if student, or any age if disabled
No age requirement

title: Child Tax Credit
description: Master the Child Tax Credit and its refundable component

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Child Tax Credit is one of the most valuable family tax benefits! Understanding the credit amount, age requirements, phase-outs, and refundable portion is essential for REG and for serving clients with children!

sections.title: Basic Child Tax Credit
sections.content:
**Credit per qualifying child:**

• **$2,000** per qualifying child under 17
• **$500** credit for other dependents (not under 17 or not qualifying child)

**Refundable portion:**
• Up to $1,700 (2024) is refundable as Additional CTC
• Requires earned income of at least $2,500

sections.title: Qualifying Child Requirements
sections.content:
**For Child Tax Credit:**

• Under age 17 at end of year
• Your qualifying child (meets CARES test)
• U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien
• Has valid SSN (not ITIN or ATIN)

**Age 17 = No CTC (but may get $500 other dependent credit)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CTC Requirements
sections.content:
**"YOUNG"** child gets CTC:

**Y**ounger than 17
**O**wn qualifying child
**U**S citizen/national/resident
**N**umber needed (valid SSN)
**G**ets $2,000 credit

**17+ = Other dependent credit only ($500)**

sections.title: Additional Child Tax Credit (Refundable)
sections.content:
**Refundable portion for those with earned income:**

• Up to $1,700 per child (2024)
• Calculated as 15% of earned income over $2,500
• Allows credit even with no tax liability

**Example:**
• Earned income: $30,000
• 15% × ($30,000 - $2,500) = $4,125
• Refundable portion limited to $1,700 per child

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SSN Requirement
sections.content:
**Child must have valid SSN!**

**ITIN (Individual Taxpayer ID Number):**
• Does NOT qualify for CTC
• Child can still be dependent
• Can get $500 other dependent credit

**Adopted child:**
• ATIN (Adoption TIN) = No CTC until SSN issued

**No SSN = No $2,000 credit!**

sections.title: Other Dependent Credit
sections.content:
**$500 credit for other dependents:**

• Qualifying relatives
• Children 17 or older
• Children with ITIN (not SSN)

**NOT refundable** (unlike CTC)

**Same phase-out thresholds as CTC**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Child Tax Credit: The $2,000 Headache Saver
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. The Child Tax Credit can feel like a maze. But trust me, nailing this now saves you major headaches later. I've seen seasoned CPAs trip up on the age cutoff or the SSN requirement. Remember, it's $2,000 per *qualifying* kid under 17 with a valid SSN. Think of it as your 'happy family' bonus, but only if you get the details right. Mess up the SSN, and the IRS will come knocking faster than you can say 'audit'.

sections.title: Age 17 Confusion: CTC vs. Other Dependent Credit
sections.content: Students often mistakenly assume that if a child is a dependent, they automatically qualify for the $2,000 Child Tax Credit. This is WRONG! The Child Tax Credit specifically requires the child to be *under* 17 at the end of the tax year. Once they turn 17, they no longer qualify for the Child Tax Credit, but they *may* qualify for the $500 credit for other dependents, assuming they meet all other dependency requirements. The key is to remember the age cutoff and the distinction between the two credits.

sections.title: Sarah's Tax Season Surprise
sections.content: Sarah is preparing a tax return for the Miller family. They have a 16-year-old, a 17-year-old, and a 20-year-old college student. Sarah initially claimed the $2,000 Child Tax Credit for both the 16 and 17-year-olds. Realizing her mistake, Sarah corrected the return. She claimed the $2,000 Child Tax Credit only for the 16-year-old and the $500 Other Dependent Credit for both the 17-year-old and the 20-year-old, highlighting the importance of precise age verification and credit eligibility assessment.

sections.title: CTC in the Real World: Client Intake Forms
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the Child Tax Credit on virtually every individual tax return with children. A thorough client intake form is crucial. Specifically, verify the child's date of birth and SSN. Many tax software programs will automatically flag potential errors related to age and SSN mismatches. This is why mastering the requirements now saves you time and reduces the risk of amended returns or IRS inquiries later. Properly documenting dependency and qualifying child status is essential for audit defense.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CTC: $2,000 per qualifying child under 17
Must have valid SSN (not ITIN) for $2,000 credit
Up to $1,700 refundable (Additional CTC)
Phase-out: $200,000 (single), $400,000 (MFJ)
Reduction: $50 per $1,000 over threshold
Other dependents: $500 (non-refundable)
Requires earned income of $2,500+ for refundable portion

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Tom and Lisa are married and have three children: Sarah (age 16), Michael (age 18 and a full-time college student), and Emily (age 10). Michael earned $5,000 during the year. Assuming Tom and Lisa meet all other requirements, for which children can they claim the Child Tax Credit in 2024?
options: Sarah and Emily
Sarah only
Emily only
Sarah, Michael, and Emily
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Child Tax Credit is available for qualifying children under the age of 17. Sarah (16) and Emily (10) meet this age requirement. Michael is 18, making him ineligible for the Child Tax Credit. He may qualify for the $500 credit for other dependents if Tom and Lisa provide more than half of his support, but he does not qualify for the Child Tax Credit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the age requirement for the Child Tax Credit and the requirements for the credit for other dependents.

sections.title: Refundable Portion of the Child Tax Credit

reveal.question: What is the maximum refundable amount of the Child Tax Credit (Additional Child Tax Credit) in 2024, and what is the minimum earned income required to claim it?
reveal.answer: The maximum refundable amount of the Child Tax Credit (Additional Child Tax Credit) in 2024 is $1,700 per qualifying child. The minimum earned income required to claim this refundable portion is $2,500.

sections.title: Child Tax Credit vs. Credit for Other Dependents

rows.label: Age Requirement
values: Under 17 at end of year
No age restriction (but must be dependent)

rows.label: Credit Amount
values: $2,000 per qualifying child
$500 per qualifying individual

rows.label: Citizenship Requirement
values: U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien
U.S. citizen, national, or resident alien

rows.label: SSN Requirement
values: Valid SSN (not ITIN or ATIN)
Valid SSN, ITIN, or ATIN

rows.label: Qualifying Child Requirement
values: Must be qualifying child per CARES test
Does not need to be a qualifying child; can be a qualifying relative

title: Earned Income Credit
description: Understand the refundable credit for low-income workers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Earned Income Credit (EIC) is the largest anti-poverty program delivered through the tax code! It's fully refundable and can provide thousands of dollars. Understanding eligibility rules and the phase-out is essential!

sections.title: Basic EIC Structure
sections.content:
**Refundable credit for low-income workers:**

• Based on earned income
• Increases with number of qualifying children
• Phases in, plateaus, then phases out
• Requires valid SSN for taxpayer, spouse, and children

**Maximum EIC (2024):**
• 0 children: ~$632
• 1 child: ~$4,213
• 2 children: ~$6,960
• 3+ children: ~$7,830

sections.title: Earned Income Requirement
sections.content:
**Must have earned income:**

**Earned income includes:**
• Wages, salaries, tips
• Self-employment income
• Union strike benefits

**NOT earned income:**
• Interest, dividends
• Pensions, Social Security
• Unemployment compensation
• Alimony

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EIC Requirements
sections.content:
**"EARNS IT"** for EIC:

**E**arned income required
**A**GI limits apply
**R**efundable (even with no tax)
**N**o excess investment income (>$11,600)
**S**SN required (valid)

**I**ncome phases out
**T**axpayer must be US citizen/resident

sections.title: Investment Income Limit
sections.content:
**Investment income cannot exceed ~$11,600 (2024)**

**Investment income includes:**
• Interest
• Dividends
• Capital gains
• Royalties
• Rental income (passive)

**Exceed limit = NO EIC!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: MFS Not Allowed
sections.content:
**Married Filing Separately = NO EIC!**

**Other disqualifications:**
• Non-resident alien
• Filing Form 2555 (foreign earned income)
• Investment income over limit
• Being qualifying child of another
• No valid SSN

**MFS is automatic disqualifier!**

sections.title: Qualifying Child for EIC
sections.content:
**Same basic tests as dependency, plus:**

• Must have valid SSN
• Must be younger than 19 (or 24 if student)
• Must have lived with you more than half year

**Different from dependency:**
• No support test for EIC!
• Child's income doesn't matter (for EIC purposes)

sections.title: Due Diligence Requirements
sections.content:
**Tax preparers must complete Form 8867:**

• Document eligibility
• Knowledge requirements
• Interview questions asked
• Records maintained

**Penalty for failure:** $560 per failure (2024)

**Applies to EIC, CTC, AOTC, and HOH status**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: EIC: The Government's Best Kept Secret?
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk EIC. Honestly, it's one of the most impactful credits out there, but also one of the most overlooked, especially by those who qualify! As a tax pro, you'll be a hero to low-income clients by helping them claim this refundable credit. Remember, it's based on *earned* income and the number of qualifying children. Don't just skim the surface; dig deep to ensure your clients get every dollar they deserve. They'll thank you for it, and your reputation will soar!

sections.title: EIC vs. Child Tax Credit: A Crucial Distinction
sections.content: Students often confuse the Earned Income Credit (EIC) with the Child Tax Credit (CTC), leading to incorrect calculations and missed opportunities. The EIC is for low-income *workers*, while the CTC is a credit for *dependent children*. While both relate to children, the EIC requires earned income, whereas the CTC has different income thresholds and requirements. Students should carefully review the eligibility criteria for each credit and ensure they understand the difference between 'earned income' for EIC and the broader definition of 'qualifying child' for both credits. Getting these mixed up is a recipe for disaster!

sections.title: Sarah's Self-Employment Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a freelance graphic designer, had a great year with $15,000 in client payments. However, she forgot to deduct her business expenses before calculating her earned income for the EIC. She mistakenly thought the gross amount was her earned income. Big mistake! Earned income from self-employment is *net* earnings (income minus expenses). By deducting her legitimate business expenses of $5,000, Sarah's earned income dropped to $10,000, impacting her potential EIC amount. Always remember the net is what counts for self-employed individuals!

sections.title: EIC Due Diligence: Protecting Yourself and Your Client
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the EIC frequently when preparing tax returns for low-to-moderate income clients. It's crucial to perform due diligence to ensure they meet the eligibility requirements, including residency, relationship, and age tests for qualifying children. The IRS scrutinizes EIC claims, and you, as the tax preparer, can face penalties for errors due to negligence or intentional disregard of rules. Document your inquiries and the information you receive from the client to protect yourself and ensure accurate reporting. Think of it as EIC insurance!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EIC is fully refundable credit for low-income workers
Requires earned income (wages, SE income)
Maximum credit increases with qualifying children
Investment income cannot exceed ~$11,600
MFS filing status = NO EIC
Valid SSN required for taxpayer and all claimed
Preparer due diligence required (Form 8867)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is MOST likely eligible for the Earned Income Credit (EIC) in 2024?
options: A single individual with $5,000 in dividend income and no earned income.
A married couple filing separately with $15,000 in wages and one qualifying child.
A single individual with $20,000 in wages and two qualifying children, all with valid SSNs.
A retired individual receiving $30,000 in Social Security benefits and no other income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is C. The EIC requires earned income (wages in this case) and is increased with the number of qualifying children. The individual, spouse (if applicable), and children must have valid SSNs. Option A has no earned income. Option B is ineligible because married couples must file jointly to claim the EIC. Option D's income is from Social Security, which is not considered earned income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the key requirements: earned income, qualifying children (if applicable), valid SSNs, and filing status.

reveal.question: List three examples of income that qualify as 'earned income' for EIC purposes.
reveal.answer: Earned income includes wages, salaries, tips, self-employment income, and union strike benefits.

rows.label: Definition
values: Compensation for services performed.
Income derived from investments or sources other than direct labor.

rows.label: Examples
values: Wages, salaries, tips, self-employment income
Interest, dividends, pensions, Social Security, unemployment compensation, alimony

rows.label: EIC Impact
values: Qualifies for EIC
Does NOT qualify for EIC

title: Education Credits: AOTC & LLC
description: Master the American Opportunity and Lifetime Learning Credits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Education is expensive, but tax credits help! The AOTC and LLC can provide significant tax savings. Understanding which credit to choose, income limits, and qualified expenses is essential for REG and client service!

sections.title: American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)
sections.content:
**Most generous education credit:**

• 100% of first $2,000 + 25% of next $2,000
• Maximum: $2,500 per student
• 40% refundable (up to $1,000)
• First 4 years of post-secondary only
• Student must be at least half-time

**Books and supplies included!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AOTC vs LLC
sections.content:
**AOTC = "A" is for AWESOME (bigger, better)**

• **A**wesome max ($2,500)
• **A**ll 4 years (undergrad)
• **A**ctually refundable (40%)

**LLC = "L" is for LIFETIME**

• **L**imited max ($2,000)
• **L**ifetime availability
• **L**ess restrictive (any courses)

sections.title: Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)
sections.content:
**More flexible but smaller:**

• 20% of first $10,000 in expenses
• Maximum: $2,000 per return (not per student!)
• Not refundable
• Any year of education (including grad school)
• One course qualifies
• No felony drug conviction restriction

sections.title: Qualified Expenses
sections.content:
**Both credits:**
• Tuition and fees
• Books (AOTC includes course materials)

**NOT qualified:**
• Room and board
• Transportation
• Insurance
• Personal expenses

**Reduce expenses by:** Tax-free scholarships, employer assistance

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cannot Double-Dip
sections.content:
**Cannot use same expenses for:**

• Both AOTC and LLC (choose one per student)
• Credit AND scholarship exclusion
• Credit AND 529 distribution

**Example:** $10,000 scholarship + $15,000 tuition
• Only $5,000 eligible for credit
• Can't claim credit on scholarship-covered expenses

**Allocate expenses wisely!**

sections.title: Who Can Claim
sections.content:
**Credit claimed by:**

• Taxpayer (for self)
• Taxpayer (for dependent)
• Taxpayer (for spouse if MFJ)

**Student claimed as dependent:**
• Parent claims credit
• Student cannot claim

**Independent student:**
• Claims own credit
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Education Credits: The Refundable Lifesaver
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk credits. The AOTC, that's your golden ticket for the first four years. Why? Because it's REFUNDABLE! That means even if your client owes zero in taxes, they can still get up to $1,000 back. Seriously, I've seen clients practically weep with joy over that refund. Don't underestimate the power of a well-applied AOTC. The LLC is great too, but it's like the consolation prize – still helpful, but not quite the same thrill.

sections.title: AOTC vs. LLC: Who Gets the Credit?
sections.content: A very common mistake is applying the Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC) when the student is actually eligible for the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC). Remember, AOTC is for the first four years of post-secondary education. Students mistakenly use the LLC because it sounds more general. Always check if the student meets the AOTC requirements (first four years, half-time student, etc.). If they do, the AOTC is almost always the better option due to its higher value and refundable portion. Don't leave money on the table!

sections.title: Sarah's Second Degree Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah is pursuing a second bachelor's degree after working for several years. She incurred $6,000 in tuition expenses. Many assume she's ineligible for the AOTC because she already has a bachelor's. However, the AOTC focuses on the first four years *of post-secondary education*, not whether a degree was previously earned. Since she's in her first four years of *this* post-secondary stint, she may qualify, assuming she meets the other requirements. Don't immediately dismiss AOTC based on prior degrees.

sections.title: Client Intake: Unlocking Education Credits
sections.content: During client intake, always ask specific questions about education expenses. Don't just ask 'Did you have any education expenses?' Dig deeper! Ask: 'What year of college are you in?' 'Are you a full-time or part-time student?' 'What were your expenses for tuition, fees, and books?' Knowing these details upfront helps you determine eligibility for the AOTC or LLC. Document everything. A few minutes of extra questioning can potentially save your clients thousands in taxes, and save you from amended returns later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AOTC: $2,500 max, 40% refundable, first 4 years only
LLC: $2,000 max per return, not refundable, unlimited years
AOTC requires at least half-time enrollment
LLC requires only one course
Both phase out at similar income levels
Cannot use same expenses for both credits
Reduce qualified expenses by tax-free scholarships

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Jacob, a first-year graduate student, paid $6,000 in qualified tuition expenses. He also has a prior felony drug conviction. Which education credit, if any, can Jacob claim?
options: American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)
Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)
Both AOTC and LLC
Neither AOTC nor LLC
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Jacob is ineligible for the AOTC because it's limited to the first four years of post-secondary education. While the LLC has no restriction on the year of education, the AOTC does impose a felony drug conviction restriction. Therefore, the LLC is the only credit Jacob can claim, assuming he meets all other requirements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the restrictions of each credit. Which credit applies to graduate school? Which credit has a drug conviction restriction?

rows.label: Maximum Credit
values: $2,500 per student
$2,000 per tax return

rows.label: Refundable?
values: No

rows.label: Years of Education
values: First 4 years of post-secondary
Any year of education (including graduate school)

rows.label: Credit Calculation
values: 20% of first $10,000 in expenses

reveal.question: What is the maximum refundable portion of the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)?
reveal.answer: The AOTC is 40% refundable, up to a maximum of $1,000 per student.

title: 🆕 H.R. 1: Enhanced Child Tax Credit
description: Understand changes to the Child Tax Credit under H.R. 1

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: H.R. 1 includes significant changes to the Child Tax Credit! Understanding these modifications—including any enhanced amounts, age limits, and refundability provisions—is important for current tax planning!

sections.title: CTC Enhancement Overview
sections.content:
**Potential H.R. 1 changes:**

• Increased credit amounts
• Modified age requirements
• Enhanced refundability
• Adjusted phase-out thresholds

**Note:** Specific provisions depend on final legislation. Review current law for exact amounts and rules.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CTC Changes
sections.content:
**"CHILD"** benefits under H.R. 1:

**C**redit amount potentially enhanced
**H**igher refundable portion possible
**I**ncome thresholds may adjust
**L**ooser or tighter rules depending on provision
**D**ependent requirements continue

**Watch for legislative updates!**

sections.title: Refundability Provisions
sections.content:
**Key consideration:**

• Refundable portion helps low-income families
• Additional CTC (ACTC) allows refund
• Earned income requirements may apply
• Maximum refundable amount matters

**Enhanced refundability = More benefit for lower-income families**

sections.title: Impact on Tax Planning
sections.content:
**Considerations:**

• Plan around phase-out thresholds
• Consider timing of income
• SSN requirements likely continue
• Withholding adjustments may be needed

**Work with current numbers but watch for changes**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Stay Current
sections.content:
**H.R. 1 provisions may be temporary!**

• Check effective dates
• Note sunset provisions
• Understand transition rules
• Prior law may return after expiration

**Exam will test law in effect for testing window**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The CTC: Congress's Favorite Toy to Tinker With
sections.content: Okay, so the Child Tax Credit. Congress loves to mess with this thing, right? One year it's this, the next year it's that. Keep an eye on the changes! Seriously, this is one of those areas where clients expect you to be the expert. Don't just assume last year's rules apply. H.R. 1 or whatever they call it this time, pay attention to the increased amounts and the refundable portion. It’s a big deal for a lot of families, and getting it wrong reflects poorly on you.

sections.title: Confusing ACTC with the Dependent Care Credit
sections.content: A common mistake is confusing the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) with the Dependent Care Credit. The ACTC is a refundable credit based on having a qualifying child and earned income, while the Dependent Care Credit is a nonrefundable credit for expenses paid for the care of a qualifying individual so you can work or look for work. Students often mix them up because both involve children. Remember, ACTC is about income and the child itself, while the Dependent Care Credit is about enabling you to work. Don't let those kids fool you!

sections.title: Sarah's Struggle: Refundability is Key
sections.content: Sarah, a single mom working part-time, earns $15,000 a year. Under pre-H.R. 1 rules, her Child Tax Credit benefit was limited due to the earned income threshold. With H.R. 1's enhanced refundability, Sarah receives a significantly larger portion of the credit as a direct refund. This extra cash helps her cover rent and childcare, highlighting the importance of understanding the refundable portion's impact on low-income families. It can be a game-changer.

sections.title: Maximizing the CTC in Tax Planning
sections.content: In practice, understanding H.R. 1's changes to the Child Tax Credit is crucial for tax planning. When meeting with clients, you'll need to analyze their income, family structure, and potential eligibility for the increased credit amounts and enhanced refundability. You'll also need to consider the phase-out thresholds and how they might impact higher-income families. By proactively assessing these factors, you can help clients maximize their tax benefits and avoid surprises during tax season. This proactive approach builds trust and positions you as a valuable advisor.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: H.R. 1 potentially enhances Child Tax Credit
Credit amount and refundability may increase
SSN requirements likely continue
Phase-out thresholds may be modified
Check current law for specific provisions
Temporary provisions may have sunset dates
Plan based on actual enacted legislation

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: H.R. 1 Enhanced Child Tax Credit: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Under potential H.R. 1 proposals concerning the Child Tax Credit (CTC), which of the following is MOST likely to directly benefit a family with very low earned income?
options: An increase in the nonrefundable portion of the CTC.
An increase in the phase-out threshold for high-income taxpayers.
Enhanced refundability provisions, allowing a larger portion of the credit to be received as a refund.
Modified age requirements that restrict the CTC to children under age 6.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Enhanced refundability provisions directly benefit low-income families because they allow a larger portion of the credit to be received as a refund, even if the family's tax liability is low or zero. The other options benefit higher-income families or restrict the credit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which provision directly translates to receiving money back, even with minimal tax liability.

reveal.question: Explain the relationship between the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) and earned income.
reveal.answer: The ACTC is the refundable portion of the Child Tax Credit. While specific rules and amounts vary by year and legislation (e.g., H.R. 1 proposals), eligibility for the ACTC often depends on meeting certain earned income requirements. A minimum level of earned income may be necessary to claim the ACTC, and the amount of the refundable credit may be limited based on the taxpayer's earned income.

sections.title: Refundability: Comparing Current Law and Potential H.R. 1

rows.label: Refundable Amount
values: Limited based on earned income and a percentage of the CTC.
Potentially increased, allowing a larger portion of the credit to be refundable, even with low or no earned income.

rows.label: Earned Income Threshold
values: A specific minimum earned income amount is required to claim the ACTC.
Potentially lowered or eliminated, making the credit more accessible to families with very low earnings.

rows.label: Impact on Low-Income Families
values: Provides some benefit, but limited by earned income requirements and the refundable amount calculation.
Potentially provides significantly greater benefit due to increased refundability and potentially relaxed earned income rules.

title: Basis Determination
description: Master the rules for determining tax basis in property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Basis is fundamental to gain/loss calculations! Whether property is purchased, inherited, or received as a gift, the basis rules differ. Getting basis wrong means calculating the wrong gain or loss. Master these rules for REG!

sections.title: What is Basis?
sections.content:
**Basis = Your investment in property**

**Used to calculate:**
• Gain or loss on sale (Amount realized - Basis = Gain/Loss)
• Depreciation
• Depletion

**General rule:** Cost basis = What you paid

sections.title: Purchased Property
sections.content:
**Cost basis includes:**

• Purchase price
• Sales tax
• Freight/delivery charges
• Installation costs
• Legal fees (related to purchase)
• Commissions paid

**Adjustments to basis:**
• + Improvements (increase basis)
• − Depreciation allowed/allowable (decrease basis)
• − Casualty loss deductions

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Basis by Acquisition
sections.content:
**"GIPsy"** for basis rules:

**G**ift = Carryover basis (generally)
**I**nheritance = FMV at death (stepped up/down)
**P**urchase = Cost
**S**ubstituted = Special rules (like-kind, etc.)

**How you GET it determines BASIS!**

sections.title: Inherited Property
sections.content:
**Stepped-up (or down) basis:**

• Basis = FMV at date of death
• OR FMV on alternate valuation date (6 months later)

**Holding period:** Always LONG-TERM (regardless of how long held)

**Exception:** Income in respect of decedent (IRD) items don't get step-up

sections.title: Gifted Property
rows: For gain: Donor basis; For loss: FMV

sections.title: Gift Basis Rules
sections.content:
**Gift when FMV < Donor basis:**

• Creates "double basis" situation
• Basis for GAIN = Donor's basis
• Basis for LOSS = FMV at gift date
• Sale price between = No gain or loss

**Example:** Donor basis $10,000, FMV $6,000
• Sale at $8,000 = No gain, no loss
• Sale at $12,000 = $2,000 gain ($12K - $10K)
• Sale at $4,000 = $2,000 loss ($6K - $4K)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Gift Tax Added to Basis
sections.content:
**Gift tax paid by donor adds to basis!**

• Add portion of gift tax attributable to appreciation
• Only if FMV > Donor's basis at gift time

**Formula:**
(FMV - Donor's basis) / FMV × Gift tax paid

**Don't add ALL gift tax—only appreciation portion!**

sections.title: Stock Basis Issues
sections.content:
**Stock splits/dividends:**
• Same total basis, spread over more shares
• Per-share basis decreases

**Stock purchases at different times:**
• FIFO (default) or specific identification
• Average cost for mutual funds

**Wash sales:**
• Add disallowed loss to new stock basis
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Basis: It's All About Keeping Good Records!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Basis can be a pain, but it's crucial. Think of it like this: the IRS wants its cut, but they don't want to overcharge you. Basis is how they figure out your taxable gain. I've seen folks try to 'wing it' with basis calculations, especially for older assets. Don't be that person! Keep detailed records of everything: purchase price, improvements, even those little fees you think don't matter. Trust me, it'll save you headaches (and potential penalties) down the road. Accurate basis is your shield against overpayment!

sections.title: Confusing Improvements with Repairs
sections.content: One common mistake is confusing improvements with repairs. Both involve spending money on an asset, but only improvements increase the basis. A repair simply maintains the asset in its current condition (like fixing a leaky faucet). An improvement increases the asset's value, useful life, or adapts it to a new use (like adding a new bathroom). Students often incorrectly add repair costs to the basis. Remember: Improvements *enhance*, repairs *maintain*. Know the difference, and your basis calculations will be much cleaner.

sections.title: Sarah's Stormy Situation
sections.content: Sarah owned a rental property damaged by a hurricane. She received insurance money to cover the repairs, but also took a casualty loss deduction on her tax return. When she later sold the property, she forgot to reduce her basis by the amount of the casualty loss deduction. This resulted in her overpaying her taxes on the sale. The takeaway: Always remember to reduce your basis for casualty loss deductions allowed or allowable, even if you didn't actually take them!

sections.title: Basis in Partnership Tax Returns
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently deal with basis when preparing partnership tax returns (Form 1065). Partners contribute assets to the partnership, and determining the correct basis of those assets is critical for calculating depreciation and potential gain or loss on future sales. For example, understanding the difference between inside and outside basis is very important. Mastering basis determination now will make you a much more valuable asset to any accounting firm or tax department dealing with partnerships.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Purchased property: Basis = Cost + acquisition expenses
Inherited property: FMV at death (stepped up/down)
Inherited property: Always long-term holding
Gifted property: Generally carryover basis
Gift with built-in loss: Double basis rules
Improvements increase basis; Depreciation decreases basis
Gift tax paid can increase donee's basis

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Alice purchased a building for $500,000. She paid $10,000 in sales tax, $5,000 for delivery, $15,000 for installation, and $2,000 in legal fees related to the purchase. During the first year, she took depreciation of $20,000. She then added an improvement costing $30,000. What is Alice's adjusted basis in the building at the end of the first year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The initial cost basis is $500,000 (purchase price) + $10,000 (sales tax) + $5,000 (delivery) + $15,000 (installation) + $2,000 (legal fees) = $532,000. Then, add the improvement of $30,000 to get $562,000. Finally, subtract the depreciation of $20,000 to get $542,000. Therefore the adjusted basis is $542,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include all costs directly related to acquiring and preparing the asset for use, and adjust for depreciation and improvements.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for determining the basis of inherited property?
reveal.answer: The basis of inherited property is generally the fair market value (FMV) at the date of death. An alternative valuation date (6 months later) can be elected, if certain conditions are met.

knowledgeCheck.question: Bob inherited stock from his mother on July 1, 2024. He sold the stock on August 1, 2024. What is the holding period for determining capital gain or loss?
options: Short-term
Long-term
Mid-term
The holding period is determined by the length of time the deceased held the asset.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Regardless of how long the beneficiary holds the inherited property, the holding period is always considered long-term.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the special rule for inherited property's holding period.

title: Capital Gains & Losses
description: Master the taxation of capital gains and losses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Capital gains taxation is a cornerstone of tax planning! Long-term gains get preferential rates while losses have limitations. Understanding the rules, netting process, and $3,000 deduction limit is essential for REG!

sections.title: Capital Asset Definition
sections.content:
**Capital asset = Property EXCEPT:**

• Inventory or stock in trade
• Property held primarily for sale to customers
• Depreciable/real property used in business (§1231)
• Accounts/notes receivable from business
• Supplies used in business
• Certain copyrights, artistic works

**"Capital assets = Investment property"** (generally)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capital Gain Rates
sections.content:
**"0-15-20"** for long-term capital gains:

**0%** = Below certain threshold (~$47,025 single)
**15%** = Middle income (most taxpayers)
**20%** = High income (>$518,900 single)

**Plus 3.8% NIIT** if income over $200K/$250K!

**Short-term = Ordinary rates (no preference)**

sections.title: Capital Gain Netting Process
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Net ST gains and ST losses
**Step 2:** Net LT gains and LT losses
**Step 3:** Net the nets

**Results:**
• Net LT gain = Preferential rates
• Net ST gain = Ordinary rates
• Net loss = Deductible up to $3,000 ($1,500 MFS)

sections.title: Capital Loss Limitation
sections.content:
**Net capital loss:**

• Deductible up to **$3,000** per year
• Excess carries forward indefinitely
• Retains character (ST or LT)
• MFS: $1,500 limit

**Use ST losses against ST gains first, then ordinary income**

sections.title: Collectibles and Section 1202 Stock
sections.content:
**Special rates apply:**

**Collectibles (art, coins, antiques):**
• Maximum 28% rate on gains

**Section 1202 QSBS (Qualified Small Business Stock):**
• Exclusion of 50%, 75%, or 100% of gain
• Must hold 5+ years
• Original issuance from qualified C corp

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Wash Sale Rule
sections.content:
**Cannot deduct loss if buy substantially identical stock within 30 days!**

**Wash sale:**
• Sell stock at loss
• Buy same stock within 30 days before OR after
• Loss disallowed
• Add disallowed loss to new stock basis

**61-day window (30 before + sale + 30 after)**

sections.title: Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)
sections.content:
**3.8% additional tax on:**

• Investment income (including capital gains)
• Lesser of: Net investment income OR excess MAGI

**Thresholds:**
• Single: $200,000
• MFJ: $250,000

**High earners may pay 23.8% on LTCG (20% + 3.8%)**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The Capital Asset Gotcha!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. That list of 'non-capital assets' looks like alphabet soup, right? The key is to remember why those exceptions exist. Think about it: inventory is a business's bread and butter, not some investment sitting around. Don't get tripped up by the 'primarily for sale' part. If you're flipping houses as your *job*, those houses are inventory, not capital assets. It's a business, not HGTV.

sections.title: ST vs. LT: Mixing Up the Rates!
sections.content: Students frequently forget that short-term capital gains are taxed at ordinary income rates, not preferential rates like long-term capital gains. This is HUGE! Imagine a client with a $10,000 short-term gain and a $5,000 long-term gain. If you accidentally tax the entire $15,000 at the long-term rate, you're underreporting their tax liability. Always keep those gains separate until you 'net the nets'!

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Snafu
sections.content: Sarah inherited stock from her grandmother and sold it six months later for a $5,000 profit. She also sold some old furniture she found in the attic at a yard sale for $200. The $5,000 from the stock is a short-term capital gain (holding period less than a year, starting from the date her grandmother passed away). The furniture sale, even though it generated $200, is a personal item and therefore not a capital asset, so it's not a taxable event.

sections.title: Capital Gains: Client Tax Planning
sections.content: In practice, you'll use this knowledge constantly when advising clients on investment strategies. For example, if a client is close to the end of the year and has significant short-term capital gains, you might suggest selling some losing investments to offset those gains and reduce their tax burden. Understanding the netting process and the different tax rates is crucial for effective tax planning and minimizing your clients' tax liabilities.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Capital assets: Generally investment property
Long-term (>1 year): 0%, 15%, or 20% rates
Short-term (≤1 year): Ordinary income rates
Net losses: Deductible up to $3,000/year
Excess losses carry forward indefinitely
Wash sale: No loss if repurchase within 30 days
NIIT: 3.8% on investment income above thresholds

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following assets would be classified as a capital asset for tax purposes?
options: Inventory held for sale to customers.
Depreciable machinery used in a business.
A copyright held by the artist who created the work.
Stock held as an investment.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Stock held as an investment is generally considered a capital asset. Inventory, depreciable property, and certain copyrights held by the creator are specifically excluded from the definition of a capital asset.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose for which the asset is held. Is it for resale, business use, or investment?

reveal.question: List at least three types of property that are *not* considered capital assets.
reveal.answer:
1. Inventory or stock in trade.
2. Property held primarily for sale to customers.
3. Depreciable/real property used in a business (§1231).
4. Accounts/notes receivable from business.
5. Supplies used in business.
6. Certain copyrights, artistic works.

knowledgeCheck.question:
An individual taxpayer has the following capital gains and losses:

*   Short-term capital gain: $5,000
*   Short-term capital loss: $2,000
*   Long-term capital gain: $8,000
*   Long-term capital loss: $10,000

What is the net capital loss that can be deducted against ordinary income?
knowledgeCheck.explanation:
Step 1: Net ST gains and losses: $5,000 - $2,000 = $3,000 ST gain
Step 2: Net LT gains and losses: $8,000 - $10,000 = $2,000 LT loss
Step 3: Net the nets: $3,000 ST gain - $2,000 LT loss = $1,000 Net ST gain. Since there is a net gain, no capital loss can be deducted.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the three-step netting process and the limitation on deducting capital losses.

title: Section 1231 Assets & Recapture
description: Understand the taxation of business property sales

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 1231 gives taxpayers the best of both worlds—gains get capital treatment, losses get ordinary treatment! But watch out for depreciation recapture. This complex area is heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: Section 1231 Property
sections.content:
**Depreciable property and real property used in business:**

• Held more than one year
• Used in trade or business
• Not inventory or held for sale

**Examples:**
• Machinery and equipment
• Buildings
• Land used in business
• Timber, coal, iron ore

sections.title: Section 1231 Netting
sections.content:
**Net all §1231 gains and losses:**

**Net GAIN:**
• Treated as long-term capital gain (preferential rate)

**Net LOSS:**
• Treated as ordinary loss (fully deductible)

**Best of both worlds!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 1231 "Hotchpot"
sections.content:
**"1231 HOTCHPOT"** rule:

**H**eld more than one year
**O**rdinary loss if net loss
**T**rade or business property
**C**apital gain if net gain
**H**otchpot all together
**P**roperty (not inventory)
**O**nly depreciable/real property
**T**ax benefits both ways!

sections.title: Section 1245 Recapture
sections.content:
**Depreciation recapture on personal property:**

• Gain taxed as ORDINARY income to extent of depreciation taken
• Remaining gain = §1231 gain

**Example:**
• Purchased equipment: $100,000
• Depreciation taken: $60,000
• Adjusted basis: $40,000
• Sold for: $80,000
• §1245 ordinary income: $40,000 (depreciation)
• §1231 gain: $0

sections.title: Section 1250 Recapture
sections.content:
**Depreciation recapture on real property:**

**Pre-1987 property:**
• Recapture excess depreciation over straight-line

**Post-1986 property (straight-line only):**
• No §1250 recapture (but "unrecaptured" rules apply)

**Unrecaptured §1250 gain:**
• Taxed at 25% maximum rate
• Applies to straight-line depreciation on real property

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: 5-Year Lookback Rule
sections.content:
**§1231 gains recharacterized as ordinary if:**

• Net §1231 losses in prior 5 years were treated as ordinary
• Recapture prevents "cherry picking"

**Example:**
• Year 1: Net §1231 loss of $10,000 (ordinary deduction)
• Year 3: Net §1231 gain of $15,000
• $10,000 treated as ordinary (recapture)
• $5,000 treated as LTCG

sections.title: Calculating Gain on Sale
sections.content:
**Order of gain recognition:**

1. **§1245 recapture** (ordinary) - Up to depreciation taken
2. **§1250 recapture** (ordinary) - Excess depreciation
3. **Unrecaptured §1250** (25%) - Remaining straight-line
4. **§1231 gain** (LTCG) - Balance

**Always recapture depreciation first!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Section 1231: The Taxpayer's Best Friend (Maybe)
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest. Tax law can be a real drag. But Section 1231? It's like the tax code threw us a bone. Think of it this way: you win big, you get capital gain rates. You lose, you get an ordinary loss deduction. It's almost too good to be true! Just remember the one-year holding period. Mess that up, and you're back to ordinary income territory. So, keep your receipts, track your dates, and let Section 1231 work its magic. It's one of the few times the tax code seems to be on your side... almost.

sections.title: Confusing 1231 with Capital Assets
sections.content: Students often confuse Section 1231 assets with regular capital assets. The key difference is that Section 1231 assets MUST be used in a trade or business and held for more than a year. A personal stock portfolio, even if held long-term, is not a Section 1231 asset. This distinction is crucial because the netting rules and potential for ordinary loss treatment apply only to 1231 assets. For example, if you sell a building used in your business at a loss, it might be an ordinary loss, but selling personal stock at a loss is always a capital loss subject to the $3,000 limitation.

sections.title: Sarah's Sore Tractor: A 1231 Tale
sections.content: Sarah runs a small farm. She sold a tractor she used for five years for $50,000. She originally bought it for $70,000 and had claimed $30,000 in depreciation. Her gain is $10,000 ($50,000 - ($70,000 - $30,000)). Here's the catch: Because she took depreciation, the $10,000 gain is all ordinary income under Section 1245 recapture. If she sold it for $80,000 instead, $30,000 would be ordinary income (recapture) and the remaining $10,000 would be a Section 1231 gain, potentially taxed at a lower rate.

sections.title: Mastering 1231: Essential for Tax Planning
sections.content: In practice, you'll use Section 1231 when preparing tax returns for businesses that sell equipment, buildings, or land. For instance, a construction company selling a used crane or a manufacturing plant selling an old warehouse. Accurately classifying these gains and losses is vital for minimizing your client's tax liability. Failing to properly apply depreciation recapture or misclassifying an asset can lead to significant errors and potential penalties. Understanding these rules is not just academic; it's a core competency for any tax professional.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: §1231 property: Business property held >1 year
Net §1231 gain = Capital gain; Net loss = Ordinary loss
§1245: Recapture ALL depreciation as ordinary (personal property)
§1250: Recapture excess depreciation (real property)
Unrecaptured §1250: 25% max rate on straight-line depreciation
5-year lookback: Prior ordinary losses recaptured from gains
Always apply recapture rules before §1231 treatment

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question:
Which of the following assets would NOT be considered a Section 1231 asset?

(a) Land held for investment purposes by a real estate developer.
(b) A warehouse used in a manufacturing business and held for 3 years.
(c) Machinery used in a manufacturing business and held for 2 years.
(d) Timber held for more than one year by a lumber company.
options: (a) Land held for investment purposes by a real estate developer.
(b) A warehouse used in a manufacturing business and held for 3 years.
(c) Machinery used in a manufacturing business and held for 2 years.
(d) Timber held for more than one year by a lumber company.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Land held for investment purposes is not used in a trade or business. Section 1231 assets must be depreciable property or real property used in a trade or business and held for more than one year. A real estate developer holding land for investment is not using the land in their trade or business.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the 'used in trade or business' requirement for Section 1231 assets.

reveal.question: How are net Section 1231 gains and losses treated for tax purposes?
reveal.answer: If the netting of all Section 1231 gains and losses results in a net gain, it is treated as a long-term capital gain, potentially benefiting from preferential tax rates. If the netting results in a net loss, it is treated as an ordinary loss, which is fully deductible against ordinary income.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company sells equipment for $75,000. The original cost of the equipment was $90,000, and accumulated depreciation is $40,000. What amount of the gain, if any, is treated as ordinary income under Section 1245?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The adjusted basis of the equipment is $90,000 - $40,000 = $50,000. The gain on the sale is $75,000 - $50,000 = $25,000. Under Section 1245, the gain is treated as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation taken, but not exceeding the gain. In this case, the entire $25,000 gain is less than the $40,000 of depreciation taken. Therefore, the entire $25,000 gain is ordinary income.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that Section 1245 recapture is limited to the lesser of the gain recognized or the accumulated depreciation.

title: Like-Kind Exchanges (Section 1031)
description: Master tax-deferred exchanges of real property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 1031 allows deferral of gain on real property exchanges! This powerful tool lets investors swap properties without immediate tax. Understanding the rules—especially TCJA changes—is essential for REG and real estate advising!

sections.title: Basic Requirements
sections.content:
**Section 1031 requirements:**

• Exchange of REAL PROPERTY only (post-TCJA)
• Held for investment or business use
• Like-kind for like-kind (broad definition)
• Not property held for sale (inventory)

**TCJA eliminated:** Personal property exchanges (equipment, vehicles, art)

sections.title: Like-Kind Definition
sections.content:
**Real property is broadly like-kind:**

✓ Rental house for apartment building
✓ Raw land for office building
✓ Commercial building for industrial property

✗ US property for foreign property (NOT like-kind)
✗ Real property for personal property

**Nature/character matters, not grade/quality**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 1031 Exchange
sections.content:
**"DEFER"** in 1031:

**D**eferral (not exclusion)
**E**xchange required (not sale/purchase)
**F**orty-five days to identify
**E**ighty (180) days to close
**R**eal property only (post-TCJA)

**Gain is DEFERRED, not eliminated!**

sections.title: Boot and Gain Recognition
sections.content:
**Boot = Non-like-kind property received:**

• Cash
• Net mortgage relief
• Other property

**Gain recognized:** Lesser of gain realized OR boot received

**Loss:** Never recognized in like-kind exchange

sections.title: Basis Calculation
sections.content:
**Basis in new property:**

FMV of new property
− Deferred gain
= Basis in new property

**Alternative formula:**
Old property basis
+ Boot paid
+ Gain recognized
− Boot received
= New property basis

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Mortgage Boot
sections.content:
**Net mortgage relief = Boot received!**

**Example:**
• Give up property with $200K mortgage
• Receive property with $150K mortgage
• Net relief: $50K = Boot received

**Can offset by paying additional cash**

**Net mortgage relief triggers gain recognition!**

sections.title: Related Party Rules
sections.content:
**Special rules for related parties:**

• If either party disposes within 2 years
• Gain is recognized (deferred gain accelerated)

**Related parties:**
• Family members
• Controlled entities (>50%)

**Exceptions:** Death, involuntary conversion
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Like-Kind Exchanges: The Devil's in the Details
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk Section 1031. Sounds fancy, right? It basically lets your client defer taxes on real estate swaps. But don't get cocky! The IRS is watching. I've seen clients try to sneak personal property in there after TCJA, hoping no one notices. It's a red flag. Remember, it's REAL PROPERTY ONLY now. Stick to the basics: investment/business use, like-kind, and no inventory. Get those down, and you're already ahead of the game. Trust me, it's easier to do it right the first time than to untangle an IRS audit later.

sections.title: Confusing 'Like-Kind' with 'Identical'
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking 'like-kind' means the properties have to be identical. Nope! The IRS is pretty lenient. A rental house for an apartment building? Like-kind! Raw land for an office building? Like-kind! The key is the nature of the property – real property held for investment or business. Students often get tripped up because they apply a stricter definition than necessary. Just remember the broad examples. However, US property for foreign property is NEVER like-kind. Keep it domestic, folks.

sections.title: Sarah's Section 1031 Slip-Up
sections.content: Sarah, a promising new associate, was preparing schedules for her first 1031 exchange. Her client exchanged a commercial building in Chicago for a vacant lot in Miami with the intention of selling the lot quickly. Sarah incorrectly treated this as a valid like-kind exchange. The problem? The vacant lot was held for sale, not for investment or business use. This seemingly small detail disqualified the entire exchange from tax deferral, creating a nasty surprise for the client.

sections.title: Section 1031: A Real-World Lifesaver
sections.content: You'll encounter Section 1031 exchanges frequently when advising clients on real estate transactions. Imagine a client wants to upgrade their rental properties without triggering a huge tax bill. A properly structured 1031 exchange can be a game-changer, allowing them to reinvest the proceeds tax-deferred. Mastering this now means you can confidently guide clients through complex transactions, potentially saving them hundreds of thousands of dollars in taxes. It's a valuable skill that will set you apart from other CPAs.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: §1031 applies only to REAL property (post-TCJA)
Must be investment or business property
45 days to identify; 180 days to close
Boot received = Gain recognized (up to realized gain)
Mortgage relief is boot (triggers gain)
Basis in new property = Old basis + gain recognized − boot received
Related party 2-year holding requirement

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following exchanges would qualify as a like-kind exchange under Section 1031?
options: An exchange of a warehouse located in the United States for a warehouse located in Canada.
An exchange of undeveloped land held for investment for a commercial building held for business use.
An exchange of a delivery truck used in a business for an office building held for investment.
An exchange of inventory held for sale to customers for a rental property.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is an exchange of undeveloped land for a commercial building. Both are real property held for investment or business use and meet the like-kind requirement. Exchanges of US property for foreign property are specifically excluded. Post-TCJA, personal property is not eligible for like-kind exchange. Property held for sale (inventory) is also excluded.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the key requirements: real property, held for investment/business, like-kind, and not inventory.

reveal.question: After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA), what type of property qualifies for a Section 1031 like-kind exchange?
reveal.answer: Only real property qualifies for a like-kind exchange under Section 1031 after the TCJA. Personal property is no longer eligible.

sections.title: Permitted vs. Not Permitted Like-Kind Exchanges

rows.label: Type of Property
values: Real Property
Personal Property

rows.label: Location
values: U.S. Property for U.S. Property
U.S. Property for Foreign Property

rows.label: Purpose
values: Held for Investment
Held for Sale (Inventory)

title: Home Sale Exclusion (Section 121)
description: Master the exclusion for gain on sale of principal residence

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The home sale exclusion is one of the most generous tax benefits available! Up to $500,000 of gain can be excluded tax-free. Understanding ownership/use tests and exceptions is essential for REG and for advising homeowners!

sections.title: Basic Exclusion Amounts
sections.content:
**Maximum exclusion:**

• **Single:** $250,000
• **Married Filing Jointly:** $500,000

**Requirements:**
• Owned for at least 2 years
• Used as principal residence for at least 2 years
• 2-year period is in the 5 years before sale
• Haven't used exclusion in past 2 years

sections.title: Ownership and Use Tests
rows: Don't need to be same 2 years

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Section 121
sections.content:
**"2-5-250"** for home sale:

**2** years ownership + use required
**5** year lookback period
**250**K excluded (single) / 500K (MFJ)

**2 in 5 = $250K free!**

sections.title: MFJ $500,000 Exclusion
sections.content:
**To get full $500,000:**

• Either spouse meets ownership test
• BOTH spouses meet use test
• Neither excluded gain in past 2 years

**If one spouse doesn't qualify:**
• Only $250,000 exclusion

sections.title: Reduced Exclusion
sections.content:
**Partial exclusion if fail due to:**

• Work relocation (50+ miles)
• Health issues
• Unforeseen circumstances

**Calculate:**
(# months qualifying / 24) × Max exclusion

**Example:** Lived 18 months, relocated for work
(18/24) × $250,000 = $187,500 exclusion

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Depreciation Recapture
sections.content:
**If home was used for business:**

• Depreciation taken is NOT excluded
• Must recapture depreciation as gain
• Subject to 25% unrecaptured §1250 rate

**Example:** Home office depreciation $10,000
• Even if gain is $200,000 (under exclusion)
• $10,000 still taxable as recapture!

**Exclusion doesn't cover depreciation!**

sections.title: Non-Qualified Use
sections.content:
**Post-2008 acquisitions:**

• Periods of non-qualified use reduce exclusion
• Non-qualified = Not principal residence

**Allocate gain:**
(Non-qualified use / Total ownership) × Gain = Non-excludable

**Exceptions:** Temporary absence, military deployment
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Home Sale Exclusion: More Than Just a Number
sections.content: Alright, folks, let's be real. The home sale exclusion seems straightforward: $250k or $500k. Easy peasy, right? Wrong! I've seen perfectly smart people trip up on the *use* test. They owned the house for five years but only lived there for 18 months in the past five years. Boom, no exclusion! So, don't gloss over the details. This isn't just memorizing numbers; it's about understanding the rules. Get it wrong, and your client's looking at a hefty tax bill. No one wants that!

sections.title: Confusing Ownership and Use: A Costly Error
sections.content: A very common mistake is assuming that if a couple is married, both spouses automatically qualify for the full $500,000 exclusion. While only *one* spouse needs to meet the ownership test, *both* spouses must meet the use test. For example, if Sarah owned the house for 3 years and lived there for 2, but Tom only lived there for 1.5 years, they're limited to a $250,000 exclusion, even if they file jointly. Always double-check the residency history of both spouses!

sections.title: The Case of the Snowbird Couple
sections.content: John and Mary sold their Florida condo for a $400,000 gain. They owned it for 10 years, but spent six months each year in their primary residence in Michigan. While they owned the condo long enough, their combined use as a principal residence only amounted to five years total, spread over the 10 years of ownership. Result? No exclusion. They should have tracked their time more carefully and possibly rented the condo out when not in use to avoid the principal residence issue.

sections.title: Home Sale Exclusion: Client Interview Gold
sections.content: In practice, the home sale exclusion comes up frequently, especially when working with retirees or those relocating. When interviewing clients, always ask probing questions about their living arrangements over the past five years. Don't just ask if they 'lived there.' Ask *how long* they lived there each year. Confirm their *primary* residence. This detailed questioning is crucial for accurately determining eligibility and maximizing tax savings. Overlooking this can lead to amended returns and unhappy clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Exclusion: $250,000 (single), $500,000 (MFJ)
Must own AND use for 2 of 5 years before sale
Both spouses must meet use test for $500K exclusion
Reduced exclusion for work, health, unforeseen circumstances
Cannot use exclusion more than once every 2 years
Depreciation recapture NOT excluded (home office)
Non-qualified use periods reduce exclusion proportionally

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary, a married couple filing jointly, sold their home in 2024 for a gain of $600,000. John owned the home for 3 years and lived in it as his primary residence for 1 year before they got married. Mary lived in the home as her primary residence for 3 years after they got married. Neither John nor Mary has used the home sale exclusion in the past 2 years. What is the maximum exclusion they can claim on their 2024 tax return?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While John meets the ownership requirement (owned for at least 2 years), he does not meet the use requirement (used as a principal residence for at least 2 years). Mary meets the use requirement, and since at least one spouse meets the ownership requirement and neither has used the exclusion in the past 2 years, they qualify for the single exclusion amount of $250,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether both spouses meet the use test and whether at least one spouse meets the ownership test.

reveal.question: What is the minimum amount of time a taxpayer must use a property as their principal residence to qualify for the Section 121 home sale exclusion?
reveal.answer: The taxpayer must have used the property as their principal residence for at least 2 years during the 5-year period ending on the date of the sale.

sections.title: Single vs. Married Filing Jointly Exclusion

rows.label: Ownership Test
values: Taxpayer must own for 2 out of 5 years before sale
Either spouse must own for 2 out of 5 years before sale

rows.label: Use Test
values: Taxpayer must use as principal residence for 2 out of 5 years before sale
Both spouses must use as principal residence for 2 out of 5 years before sale

rows.label: Prior Exclusion
values: Taxpayer cannot have excluded gain in past 2 years
Neither spouse can have excluded gain in past 2 years

title: Above-the-Line Deductions
description: Master adjustments to income that reduce AGI

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Above-the-line deductions (adjustments to income) are valuable because EVERYONE can claim them—regardless of whether you itemize! These reduce AGI, which can unlock other tax benefits. Know these cold for REG!

sections.title: What are Above-the-Line Deductions?
sections.content:
**Deductions subtracted from gross income to arrive at AGI:**

• Available to ALL taxpayers (itemizers AND standard deduction)
• Listed on Schedule 1 (Form 1040)
• Reduce AGI, which affects many other calculations

**AGI affects:** Medical deduction floor, passive loss limits, IRA deduction, education credits

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Above-the-Line
sections.content:
**"SHEA" deductions:**

**S**elf-employment (tax, health, retirement)
**H**SA contributions
**E**ducator expenses
**A**limony (pre-2019 only)

**Plus: Student loan interest, IRA contributions**

sections.title: Self-Employment Deductions
sections.content:
**Three key SE deductions:**

1. **50% of SE tax** - Deduct half of 15.3% paid
2. **SE health insurance** - 100% of premiums for self and family
3. **SE retirement** - SEP, SIMPLE, Solo 401(k)

**Health insurance limit:** Cannot exceed net SE income

**Retirement limit:** Based on plan type and income

sections.title: Student Loan Interest
sections.content:
**Deduction for interest on qualified education loans:**

• Maximum: $2,500 per year
• Available even if you don't itemize
• Loan must be for taxpayer, spouse, or dependent
• Phaseout based on MAGI

**2024 phaseout:**
• Single: $80,000-$95,000
• MFJ: $165,000-$195,000

sections.title: Health Savings Account (HSA)
sections.content:
**Triple tax benefit:**

1. Contributions are above-the-line deduction
2. Earnings grow tax-free
3. Distributions for medical expenses tax-free

**Requirements:**
• Must have high-deductible health plan (HDHP)
• Cannot be enrolled in Medicare
• Cannot be claimed as dependent

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: IRA Deduction Limits
sections.content:
**Traditional IRA deduction may be LIMITED if:**

• Covered by employer retirement plan, AND
• MAGI exceeds threshold

**2024 phaseout (if covered):**
• Single: $77,000-$87,000
• MFJ: $123,000-$143,000

**Spouse not covered:** Higher phaseout
**Not covered by plan:** Full deduction allowed
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Schedule 1 Shenanigans: My Tax Season Confession
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. Schedule 1 isn't exactly thrilling reading. But trust me, nailing these above-the-line deductions is crucial. I remember my first tax season – I completely missed a client's SEP contribution deduction and had to file an amended return. The client wasn't happy! Don't be *that* CPA. Understanding these adjustments to income can save your clients money and save you from embarrassing errors. Pay attention, and you'll be thanking me (and your clients will be thanking you too) come April 15th!

sections.title: SE Tax Deduction: The 15.3% Trap!
sections.content: A common mistake students make is deducting the *entire* self-employment tax liability. Remember, you only deduct *one-half* of the self-employment tax. For example, if your total SE tax is $10,000 (based on your Schedule SE), you can deduct $5,000 on Schedule 1. The confusion arises because you *pay* the full 15.3%, but the *deduction* is limited to half. Don't let this simple rule trip you up on the exam – or in practice!

sections.title: Sarah's Solo 401(k) Surprise
sections.content: Sarah, a freelance graphic designer, contributed $20,000 to her Solo 401(k) this year. She's excited about the tax savings! However, she forgot that the deduction is capped based on her net self-employment income. Even though she contributed $20,000, her deduction will be limited to what she actually earned. This highlights the importance of understanding the limitations on these deductions, not just the fact that they exist.

sections.title: Real-World Impact: AGI and Client Savings
sections.content: In practice, mastering above-the-line deductions directly impacts client outcomes. A lower AGI can unlock significant tax benefits, such as increased eligibility for itemized deductions (medical expenses, for example) and education credits. When preparing a tax return, always prioritize identifying and maximizing these deductions. It demonstrates your expertise and builds trust with your clients, leading to long-term relationships and referrals.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Above-the-line deductions available to ALL taxpayers
Reduce AGI, which affects other tax benefits
SE: 50% of SE tax, health insurance, retirement
Student loan interest: Up to $2,500 (phaseout applies)
HSA: Triple tax benefit (deduction, growth, distribution)
IRA deduction may be limited if covered by employer plan
Educator expenses: $300 per qualifying teacher

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is a true statement regarding above-the-line deductions?
options: Above-the-line deductions are only available to taxpayers who itemize deductions.
Above-the-line deductions are subtracted from adjusted gross income (AGI) to arrive at taxable income.
Above-the-line deductions are reported on Schedule A (Form 1040).
Above-the-line deductions reduce a taxpayer's adjusted gross income (AGI).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that above-the-line deductions reduce AGI. They are subtracted from gross income to arrive at AGI. They are available to all taxpayers, regardless of whether they itemize, and are reported on Schedule 1 (Form 1040).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the definition of 'above-the-line' in relation to the income statement and AGI.

reveal.question: How much of self-employment tax is deductible, and where is it deducted?
reveal.answer: One-half (50%) of the self-employment tax is deductible. It is an above-the-line deduction, meaning it's deducted from gross income to arrive at AGI. It is reported on Schedule 1 (Form 1040).

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, a self-employed individual, paid $8,000 in health insurance premiums for herself and her family. Her net self-employment income was $6,000. What is the maximum amount of self-employment health insurance premium that Sarah can deduct as an above-the-line deduction?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $6,000. The self-employed health insurance deduction is limited to the taxpayer's net self-employment income. Therefore, Sarah can only deduct up to $6,000, even though she paid $8,000 in premiums.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the limitation on the self-employed health insurance deduction based on net self-employment income.

title: Itemized Deductions
description: Master Schedule A deductions including SALT, mortgage interest, and charitable

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Itemized deductions on Schedule A compete with the standard deduction! After TCJA doubled the standard deduction, fewer taxpayers itemize—but understanding the rules is still essential for tax planning and the REG exam!

sections.title: Itemize or Standard Deduction?
sections.content:
**2024 Standard Deduction:**

• Single: $14,600
• MFJ: $29,200
• Head of Household: $21,900
• Additional if 65+ or blind: $1,550-$1,950

**Itemize ONLY if itemized deductions exceed standard!**

sections.title: Medical Expenses
sections.content:
**Deduct ONLY amounts exceeding 7.5% of AGI:**

**Deductible:**
• Doctor, dental, hospital bills
• Prescription drugs
• Health insurance premiums (not pre-tax)
• Long-term care (with limits)
• Medical transportation

**NOT deductible:** Cosmetic surgery, gym memberships, OTC drugs

sections.title: State and Local Taxes (SALT)
sections.content:
**$10,000 cap on SALT deduction:**

**Includes:**
• State income tax OR sales tax (choose one)
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes

**Cap:** $10,000 total ($5,000 if MFS)

**H.R. 1 Note:** Cap may change after July 1, 2026

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SALT Cap
sections.content:
**"10K is the SALT limit"**

**S**tate income tax (OR sales)
**A**nd
**L**ocal property
**T**axes

**Total capped at $10,000!**

**High-tax state? Hurts more.**

sections.title: Mortgage Interest
sections.content:
**Deductible on acquisition debt up to $750,000:**

**Qualified residence:**
• Principal residence AND
• One other residence (vacation home)

**Post-TCJA rules:**
• No deduction for home equity interest (unless used to improve home)
• $750,000 limit for loans after 12/15/2017
• Pre-12/15/2017 loans: $1,000,000 limit

sections.title: Charitable Contributions
sections.content:
**AGI limits depend on type:**

• Cash to public charity: 60% of AGI
• Property to public charity: 30% of AGI
• Capital gain property: 30% of AGI
• Private foundations: 30%/20%

**Excess carries forward 5 years**

**Substantiation required:** Receipt for $250+ donations

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Charitable Property Deduction
sections.content:
**Deduction amount depends on property type:**

**Capital gain property held >1 year:**
• Deduct FMV (no gain recognized)
• 30% AGI limit

**Ordinary income property:**
• Deduct LESSER of basis or FMV
• Appreciation not deductible

**Watch what's being donated!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Schedule A: It's All About the Math (and the Receipts!)
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Schedule A can feel like a giant scavenger hunt for deductions. I've seen clients try to deduct everything from their dog's vet bills to their 'lucky' socks. Remember, the IRS isn't buying it. Focus on the big hitters – SALT, mortgage interest, and charitable contributions. And for the love of all that is holy, keep your receipts! A shoebox full of crumpled papers won't cut it. Get organized, know your limits (especially that pesky $10,000 SALT cap), and you'll be golden.

sections.title: 7.5% AGI Threshold: The Medical Expense Minefield
sections.content: Students often forget that you can ONLY deduct medical expenses exceeding 7.5% of your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI). It's not a dollar-for-dollar deduction! For example, if your AGI is $60,000, the threshold is $4,500. If you have $5,000 in medical expenses, you can only deduct $500. Failing to calculate this threshold correctly is a HUGE mistake that will cost you points on the exam and credibility in practice. Double-check that AGI and threshold calculation!
