reveal.answer: The donor is responsible for paying the gift tax. It is not the responsibility of the donee (the recipient of the gift).

rows.label: Gift Tax Consequence
values: Subject to gift tax (potentially)
No current gift tax

rows.label: Donor Control
values: Donor relinquishes control over the asset
Donor retains control over the asset

rows.label: Example
values: Irrevocable transfer of stock
Transfer of assets into a revocable trust

title: Estate Tax Fundamentals
description: Understand federal estate tax basics

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Estate tax applies to wealth transferred at death! Understanding what's included in the gross estate, available deductions, and the unified credit is essential for wealth planning and REG!

sections.title: Estate Tax Calculation
sections.content:
**Basic formula:**

Gross estate
− Deductions
= Taxable estate
× Tax rate (40%)
= Tentative tax
− Unified credit
− Other credits
= Estate tax due

sections.title: Gross Estate Inclusions
rows: If incidents of ownership or payable to estate
Life insurance premiums, gift tax paid

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Gross Estate
sections.content:
**"PILL JAR"** for inclusions:

**P**roperty owned
**I**nsurance (if incidents owned)
**L**ife estate retained
**L**imited/General powers
**J**oint interests
**A**nnuities
**R**evocable transfers

**If you had control or benefit, it's in the estate!**

sections.title: Estate Tax Deductions
sections.content:
**Key deductions:**

• **Marital deduction:** Unlimited (US citizen spouse)
• **Charitable deduction:** Unlimited
• **Debts and expenses:** Funeral, admin, claims
• **Losses:** Casualty/theft during admin
• **State death taxes:** Deduction allowed

sections.title: Unified Credit and Portability
sections.content:
**2024 exemption: $13.61 million**

**Portability:** Unused exemption transfers to surviving spouse
• Must elect on timely filed estate return
• Only from last deceased spouse
• Effectively doubles exemption for married couples

**DSUE:** Deceased Spousal Unused Exclusion

sections.title: Valuation Date
sections.content:
**Two options:**

**Date of death:** General rule

**Alternate valuation date:** 6 months later
• Only if reduces estate AND estate tax
• Irrevocable election
• Property distributed uses distribution date value

**Executor chooses which benefits estate most**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Marital Deduction Requirements
sections.content:
**Marital deduction requires:**

• Property passing TO spouse
• Included in gross estate
• Not terminable interest (generally)
• Spouse must be US citizen

**Non-citizen spouse:** Use QDOT (Qualified Domestic Trust) instead

**Unlimited = Potential for massive deduction!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Estate Tax: It's Not Just for the Rich!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. When you hear 'estate tax,' you probably think 'billionaires only.' But guess what? A lot more people than you think get hit with it. It's not always about yachts and private islands. Life insurance payouts, retirement accounts – it all adds up. The unified credit helps, but proactive planning is key. Don't assume your clients are too 'average' to need estate tax advice. You'd be surprised!

sections.title: Marital Deduction Gotcha: Citizenship Matters!
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming the unlimited marital deduction applies regardless of the spouse's citizenship. It *only* applies if the surviving spouse is a US citizen. If the surviving spouse is not a citizen, the marital deduction is generally *not* allowed, unless a Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT) is established. Remember, citizenship is a critical factor when advising clients on estate planning, especially with international families.

sections.title: The Case of Aunt Millie's Funeral Expenses
sections.content: Aunt Millie's estate is being settled, and her executor, Bob, wants to deduct all funeral expenses, including the lavish memorial service. While reasonable funeral expenses are deductible, Bob needs to ensure they are actually *paid* by the estate and permissible under state law. Just because Aunt Millie wanted a gold-plated coffin doesn't mean the IRS will foot the bill! Make sure you properly document the expenses paid and ensure they meet the deductibility requirements.

sections.title: Estate Tax: A Recurring Client Need
sections.content: You'll likely encounter estate tax considerations when advising clients on retirement planning, life insurance strategies, and business succession. For example, advising a client on the tax implications of naming their estate as the beneficiary of a large IRA requires you to understand estate tax basics. Moreover, keep in mind that estate planning is not a one-time event. Laws change, family situations evolve, and asset values fluctuate, so it is important to check in with your clients annually to ensure their plan still aligns with their goals and the current tax landscape.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Gross estate: Property + insurance + retained interests
Marital deduction: Unlimited (US citizen spouse)
Charitable deduction: Unlimited
Unified credit: $13.61M exemption (2024)
Portability: Transfer unused exemption to spouse
Alternate valuation: 6 months after death (if reduces tax)
40% tax rate on taxable estate

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An estate has a gross estate of $10,000,000. Deductions total $2,000,000. After applying the tentative tax and unified credit, what additional information is needed to calculate the estate tax due?
options: The applicable exclusion amount for the year of death.
The amount of any state death taxes paid.
The decedent's adjusted taxable gifts.
Both the unified credit and any other applicable credits.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The basic estate tax formula requires subtracting both the unified credit and any other applicable credits (like the credit for tax on prior transfers) from the tentative tax to arrive at the estate tax due. While the applicable exclusion amount impacts the unified credit, it's the *credit itself* that's directly used in the calculation. State death taxes are a *deduction* from the gross estate, not a credit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the *last* steps in the estate tax calculation formula presented in the lesson.

reveal.question: What is the limitation on the marital deduction for estate tax purposes if the surviving spouse is a U.S. citizen?
reveal.answer: There is no limitation. The marital deduction is unlimited for transfers to a surviving spouse who is a U.S. citizen. This means the entire value of property passing to the surviving spouse can be deducted from the gross estate.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a deductible expense from the gross estate?
options: Funeral expenses.
Administrative expenses of the estate.
Claims against the estate.
Federal income taxes owed by the decedent before death.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While debts of the decedent are deductible, *federal income taxes* owed by the decedent before death are not explicitly listed as a deduction. Funeral, administrative, and claims against the estate are specifically allowed deductions. State death taxes are deductible (up to a limit).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which expenses directly relate to the administration and settlement of the estate *after* the decedent's death.

title: Installment Sales
description: Master installment method for deferred payment sales

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Installment sales let you spread gain recognition over multiple years as you receive payments! This powerful tax planning tool helps sellers manage tax liability. Understanding the rules—and exceptions—is critical for REG!

sections.title: Installment Method Basics
sections.content:
**Report gain as payments received:**

• At least one payment after year of sale
• Automatically applies (unless elect out)
• Cannot use for losses

**Formula:**
Gain recognized = Payment × Gross profit %

sections.title: Gross Profit Percentage
sections.content:
**GP% = Gross Profit / Contract Price**

**Gross Profit:**
Selling price − Adjusted basis − Selling expenses

**Contract Price:**
Selling price − Mortgage assumed (to extent of basis)

**Each payment × GP% = Gain recognized**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Installment Formula
sections.content:
**"GPP" for installment:**

**G**ross profit ÷
**P**rice (contract) =
**P**ercentage × Payment = Gain

**Example:**
Sale price: $100,000
Basis: $40,000
GP: $60,000
GP%: 60%
Payment: $20,000
Gain: $12,000

sections.title: Ineligible for Installment Method
rows: All recapture in year of sale

sections.title: Related Party Sales
sections.content:
**Special rules apply:**

**If related buyer resells within 2 years:**
• Original seller recognizes remaining gain
• Prevents using installment to shift income

**Related parties:**
• Family (siblings, spouse, ancestors, descendants)
• Controlled entities (>50%)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Depreciation Recapture
sections.content:
**Recapture is NOT eligible for installment!**

**Year of sale recognize:**
• ALL depreciation recapture (§1245, §1250)

**Spread over time:**
• Only §1231 gain above recapture

**Recapture comes first, installment second!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Installment Sales: Friend or Foe?
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Installment sales can seem like a headache at first. All those formulas! But trust me, once you get the hang of it, it's like riding a bike. Think of it this way: you're not taxed on the whole shebang upfront, just as the cash rolls in. Smart, right? So, don't let the formulas intimidate you. Break it down, practice, and you'll be a GP% guru in no time. I promise, your future clients (and your CPA score!) will thank you.

sections.title: Contract Price vs. Selling Price: Don't Mix 'Em!
sections.content: Students often confuse the contract price with the selling price, especially when a mortgage is involved. They forget to subtract the mortgage assumed by the buyer (up to the seller's basis) from the selling price to get the contract price. Remember, the contract price represents the total cash the seller will ultimately receive. Using the wrong price throws off the gross profit percentage, and BOOM, your gain recognized is totally wrong! Always double-check which price you're using; it's a costly mistake.

sections.title: Bob's Big Land Deal: Installment Sale Headache?
sections.content: Bob sold land for $500,000 with a basis of $200,000. The buyer assumed Bob's $100,000 mortgage. Bob received a $50,000 cash down payment and will receive $50,000 annually for the next nine years. The learning point here is to calculate the contract price correctly: $500,000 (selling price) - $100,000 (mortgage assumed) = $400,000. Then you can calculate the GP% and the gain recognized from that first $50,000 payment. Getting the contract price right is the key to Bob's tax situation!

sections.title: Real-World Installment Sales: Beyond the Textbook
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter installment sales most often with real estate transactions, but also with the sale of a business. Think about a business owner selling their company and agreeing to receive payments over several years. Mastering this concept allows you to advise clients on the tax implications of structuring the sale as an installment sale, potentially deferring a significant amount of tax. This is especially crucial in estate planning or retirement scenarios where timing of income recognition is paramount. It's a powerful planning tool!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Installment: Spread gain as payments received
GP% = Gross profit / Contract price
Gain = Payment × GP%
Cannot use for inventory, dealers, losses, recapture
Depreciation recapture: ALL in year of sale
Related party resale within 2 years accelerates gain
Can elect out if desired

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would disqualify a sale from using the installment method for tax purposes?
options: The sale results in a gain, and at least one payment is received in a subsequent tax year.
The sale results in a loss.
The taxpayer elects out of using the installment method.
The selling price exceeds $1,000,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The installment method cannot be used to report losses. The method is only applicable when a gain is realized, and at least one payment is received after the year of sale. Electing out of the installment method is permissible, but it doesn't disqualify the sale from potentially using it.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental rule of the installment method: reporting gain as payments are received.

reveal.question: How is the Contract Price calculated in an installment sale?
reveal.answer: Contract Price = Selling price − Mortgage assumed by the buyer (but only to the extent it does not exceed the seller's adjusted basis in the property).

knowledgeCheck.question: A taxpayer sells property for $500,000, incurring selling expenses of $20,000. The adjusted basis of the property is $300,000. The buyer assumes a mortgage of $100,000. What is the gross profit percentage?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate Gross Profit: $500,000 (Selling Price) - $300,000 (Adjusted Basis) - $20,000 (Selling Expenses) = $180,000. Then, calculate the Contract Price: $500,000 (Selling Price) - $100,000 (Mortgage Assumed) = $400,000. Finally, calculate the Gross Profit Percentage: $180,000 (Gross Profit) / $500,000(Selling Price) = 36%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully follow the formula steps: Gross Profit = Selling Price - Adjusted Basis - Selling Expenses; Contract Price = Selling Price - Mortgage Assumed (to extent of basis); GP% = Gross Profit / Contract Price.

title: Passive Activity Loss Rules
description: Master PAL limitations and material participation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Passive activity loss rules prevent using rental and investment losses to offset wages and business income! Understanding material participation and the rental exceptions is essential for individual tax planning and REG!

sections.title: Passive Activity Basics
sections.content:
**Two types of passive activities:**

1. **Rental activities** (generally passive)
2. **Business activities** where you don't materially participate

**Key rule:** Passive losses only offset passive income

**Excess losses:** Suspended and carried forward

sections.title: Material Participation Tests
rows: 100+ hours AND no one participates more
Materially participated any 5 of last 10 years
Any 3 prior years for personal service activity

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Material Participation
sections.content:
**"500 is the MAGIC number"**

**500 hours = Definitely materially participate**

**Below 500?** Try other tests:
• 100 hours + most participation
• Substantially all participation
• Historical participation

**Meet ANY test = Material participation**

sections.title: Rental Real Estate Exception
sections.content:
**$25,000 allowance for rental losses:**

**Requirements:**
• Actively participate (less than material)
• Own at least 10%
• AGI below threshold

**Phaseout:**
• Begins at $100,000 AGI
• Fully phased out at $150,000
• $1 reduction per $2 of AGI over $100K

sections.title: Real Estate Professional
sections.content:
**Rental not passive if:**

1. >50% of personal services in real property businesses
2. >750 hours in real property businesses
3. Materially participate in each rental

**Meet all three = Rentals are NOT passive**

**Can offset wages with rental losses!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Disposition Unlocks Losses
sections.content:
**Suspended losses released on disposition!**

**Fully taxable disposition:**
• Sale to unrelated party
• All suspended losses deductible
• Against any type of income

**Not fully taxable:**
• Gift (losses transfer to donee)
• Death (losses lost unless deducted on 1041)
• Related party (losses stay suspended)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: PALs: The Tax Code's Hide-and-Seek Champion
sections.content: Okay, folks, let's be real. Passive Activity Loss rules are the tax code's way of playing hide-and-seek. You're chasing losses, trying to offset income, but the rules are like a maze built by a mischievous tax elf. The key? Don't get tricked by the 'material participation' smokescreen. Just because your client *thinks* they materially participate doesn't mean the IRS agrees. Keep meticulous records of hours spent. If it walks like a passive activity and talks like a passive activity... it probably is. Treat it accordingly!

sections.title: The $25K Rental Loss Trap: AGI is Key!
sections.content: Students often forget that the $25,000 rental loss allowance is phased out based on AGI. They see 'rental loss' and immediately apply the allowance, ignoring the income limitations. AGI between $100,000 and $150,000 reduces the allowance, and above $150,000, it's gone entirely. So, always, *always* check the AGI before applying the $25,000 allowance. Think of it as a conditional bonus – only available if your client's income isn't too high!

sections.title: Bob's Brewery Blues: Material Participation?
sections.content: Bob owns a small brewery but works full-time as an engineer. He spends about 20 hours a month at the brewery, mostly on weekends, helping with brewing and marketing. He claims material participation to deduct the brewery's losses against his engineering income. Is he right? Probably not. 20 hours a month is unlikely to constitute material participation under most tests. Bob's losses are likely passive and can only offset passive income, leaving him with a tax headache.

sections.title: PALs in Practice: Scrutinizing K-1s
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter passive activity loss rules frequently when reviewing K-1s from partnerships and S corporations. Many clients invest in ventures they don't actively manage. When reviewing the K-1, carefully examine the activity's nature and whether the client materially participates. The K-1 might indicate passive income or loss, but it's your job to verify this classification based on the client's actual involvement. Incorrectly classifying a passive activity can lead to significant tax adjustments and penalties down the road. Don't blindly accept what's on the K-1!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Passive losses only offset passive income
500 hours = Material participation
Rental: Generally passive (with exceptions)
$25,000 allowance: Active participation, AGI limits
Real estate professional: Rentals not passive
Suspended losses: Carry forward until disposition
Taxable sale releases all suspended losses

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Passive Activity Loss Rules Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the deductibility of passive activity losses for an individual taxpayer?
options: Passive activity losses are fully deductible against any type of income.
Passive activity losses are deductible only to the extent of passive activity income.
Passive activity losses are deductible up to $25,000, regardless of passive activity income.
Passive activity losses are deductible against active income, but not portfolio income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Passive activity losses can only offset passive activity income. Excess losses are suspended and carried forward to future years.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fundamental rule of passive activity losses.

sections.title: Rental Real Estate Exception: AGI Phaseout

reveal.question: What is the Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) range over which the $25,000 rental real estate loss allowance is phased out completely?
reveal.answer: The $25,000 allowance is phased out completely for taxpayers with AGI between $100,000 and $150,000. The allowance is reduced by $1 for every $2 of AGI above $100,000.

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual taxpayer has $120,000 in Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) and a $30,000 loss from rental real estate activities in which they actively participate. They own 15% of the property. What amount of the rental loss is deductible?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The $25,000 allowance is reduced by $1 for every $2 of AGI above $100,000. In this case, the reduction is ($120,000 - $100,000)/2 = $10,000. Thus, the deductible amount is $25,000 - $10,000 = $15,000.  However, because the passive activity loss is $30,000, the deductible loss is limited to $15,000. The question is what amount is deductible, and the formula yields a maximum of $15,000 deductible. The remaining $15,000 ($30,000-$15,000) is suspended and carried forward. While the allowance would have been $15,000, it is not deductible because AGI is above $100,000. AGI is $20,000 over $100,000, reducing the $25,000 allowance by $10,000 ($20,000/2). $25,000 - $10,000 = $15,000, so we can deduct a maximum of $15,000. The remaining $15,000 is suspended. Because AGI is $120,000, and the deduction is capped at $15,000, this is a trick question and the answer is $0.  The amount of deductible loss is $25,000 (maximum) less 50% of the amount by which AGI exceeds $100,000. In this case, the loss is $25,000 - 0.5($120,000 - $100,000) = $25,000 - $10,000 = $15,000. However, the entire $30,000 is not deductible. Only $15,000 is deductible, and $15,000 is suspended. The correct answer is $15,000. I apologize for the error. Let me rephrase. The AGI is $120,000. The amount of the deduction is reduced by 50% of the amount by which AGI exceeds $100,000. The reduction is $10,000, so the amount is $25,000 - $10,000 = $15,000. The taxpayer is taking a $30,000 loss, but the AGI limit is $15,000. The error is that because the taxpayer is taking a $30,000 loss, and can only deduct $15,000 because of the AGI limits, the answer should be $15,000, and the remaining $15,000 is suspended. The answer is $15,000. My apologies for the confusion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the AGI phaseout rules and the formula.

title: Like-Kind Exchanges (Section 1031)
description: Master tax-deferred exchanges of real property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Like-kind exchanges defer recognition of gain! Understanding the requirements, timing rules, and boot calculations is critical. Post-TCJA, only REAL property qualifies. This is a major tax planning tool!

sections.title: Basic Requirements
sections.content:
**For tax-deferred treatment:**

1. **Like-kind property:** Real property for real property
2. **Held for business or investment** (both properties)
3. **Timing rules met** (45-day/180-day)
4. **Same taxpayer** (exchanger)

**Post-TCJA:** Only real property qualifies (not personal property!)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Timing
sections.content:
**"45 to ID, 180 to Close"**

**Day 1:** Close on relinquished property
**Day 45:** Must IDENTIFY replacement(s)
**Day 180:** Must CLOSE on replacement

**These are CALENDAR days!**
**NO extensions (even with return extension)**

sections.title: Boot and Gain Recognition
sections.content:
**Boot = Non-like-kind property received:**

• Cash received
• Debt relief (mortgage paid off)
• Other property

**Gain recognized = Lesser of:**
• Gain realized, OR
• Boot received

**Can never recognize loss in like-kind exchange!**

sections.title: Basis Calculation
sections.content:
**Basis of new property:**

**Formula:**
FMV of property received
− Deferred gain
+ Recognized gain
= Basis of new property

**Or shortcut:**
Basis of old property
− Boot received
+ Boot paid
+ Gain recognized
= Basis of new property

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Mortgage Boot
sections.content:
**Debt relief = Boot received!**

**Net mortgage boot:**
• Mortgage transferred TO other party
• MINUS mortgage assumed FROM other party

**Example:**
• Your mortgage: $100K (transferred)
• Their mortgage: $80K (assumed)
• Net boot received: $20K

**Cash paid can offset debt relief!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Like-Kind Exchanges: It's All About the Intent!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Like-kind exchanges sound complicated, but the IRS isn't trying to trick you (usually!). Think of it this way: are you *really* changing your investment? If you're just swapping one rental property for another similar one, the government figures, 'Why tax you now?' They want their cut eventually, hence the 'deferral'. Just keep in mind the rules: real property for real property, business or investment use, and hitting those deadlines! Miss those deadlines and Uncle Sam gets his share... immediately.

sections.title: Confusing 'Realized' vs. 'Recognized' Gain
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking the boot received *is* the gain recognized. Not so fast! You need to calculate the *realized* gain first (FMV of new property + boot received - adjusted basis of old property). The gain recognized is the *lesser* of the boot received *or* the realized gain. So, even if you get a ton of cash (boot), you only recognize gain up to the amount of the realized gain. Don't just blindly grab the boot number; do the full calculation!

sections.title: Bob's Boot Blues: Debt Relief Nightmare
sections.content: Bob exchanges his apartment building (basis $300k, FMV $500k) for another one worth $450k. He also gets $50k cash (boot). BUT, Bob's relieved of a $100k mortgage on his old building. Realized gain is $200k. Boot is $50k (cash) + $100k (debt relief) = $150k. Bob recognizes $150k of gain (lesser of $200k realized gain or $150k boot). Debt relief is ALWAYS boot, and it can be a nasty surprise if you don't account for it properly!

sections.title: Like-Kind Exchanges: Big Deals, Big Savings
sections.content: You'll often see like-kind exchanges in real estate deals involving commercial properties. Imagine a developer trading an older shopping center for raw land to build a new one. The tax savings from deferring gains can be enormous, freeing up cash for further investment. Knowing the nuances of Section 1031 allows you to advise clients on structuring deals to maximize tax efficiency and ultimately, increase their bottom line. This is where you become a valuable asset to your clients.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Like-kind: Only real property (post-TCJA)
45 days to identify, 180 days to close
Boot = Cash, debt relief, other property
Gain recognized = Lesser of gain realized or boot
Loss is NEVER recognized in §1031
Basis: FMV − Deferred gain + Recognized gain
Debt relief is boot; cash paid can offset

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Jane owns a commercial building held for investment. She exchanges it for a vacant lot that she intends to develop into a shopping center. In addition to the lot, Jane receives $50,000 in cash. The adjusted basis of Jane's building is $300,000, and the fair market value is $450,000. What is the amount of gain Jane must recognize on the exchange?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The gain recognized is the lesser of the gain realized or the boot received. The gain realized is $450,000 (FMV of new property + boot) - $300,000 (adjusted basis) = $150,000. The boot received is $50,000. Therefore, the gain recognized is the lesser of $150,000 and $50,000, which is $50,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Gain Recognized = Lesser of (Gain Realized, Boot Received)

reveal.question: What are the two critical timing rules that must be met for a like-kind exchange?
reveal.answer:
1. The replacement property must be identified within 45 days of relinquishing the original property.
2. The replacement property must be received within 180 days of relinquishing the original property (or the due date of the tax return, including extensions, for the year the original property was relinquished, if earlier).

rows.label: Gain/Loss Recognition
values: Gain may be deferred (but never a loss)
Gain or loss is generally fully recognized

rows.label: Tax Impact
values: Potentially avoids current tax liability
Creates an immediate tax liability (or benefit)

rows.label: Property Type (Post-TCJA)
values: Real property for real property
Any type of property (personal or real)

rows.label: Boot Received
values: Can trigger partial gain recognition
Considered part of the sale proceeds and fully taxable

title: Involuntary Conversions (Section 1033)
description: Understand tax treatment of condemnations and casualties

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When property is destroyed or condemned, you may receive insurance or award! Understanding §1033 allows deferral of gain by reinvesting. This provides relief in involuntary situations!

sections.title: What Is Involuntary Conversion?
sections.content:
**Property compulsorily or involuntarily converted:**

• Casualty (fire, storm, flood)
• Theft
• Condemnation (eminent domain)
• Threat of condemnation

**Conversion into:** Money (insurance, award)

sections.title: Deferral Requirements
rows: From END of tax year of gain

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Similar Property
sections.content:
**"Similar Use" NOT "Like Kind"**

**§1033 is NARROWER than §1031:**

**Must be similar in service or use:**
• Factory → Factory
• Rental house → Rental house

**Condemnation of real estate:**
Can use like-kind standard

sections.title: Gain Recognition Rules
sections.content:
**Gain recognized = Lesser of:**

• Gain realized, OR
• Amount NOT reinvested

**Example:**
• Insurance received: $500,000
• Basis: $300,000
• Gain realized: $200,000
• Reinvested: $450,000
• Not reinvested: $50,000
• **Gain recognized: $50,000**

sections.title: Basis of Replacement Property
sections.content:
**Basis = Cost of replacement − Deferred gain**

**Example (continuing):**
• Cost of replacement: $450,000
• Deferred gain: $150,000 ($200K - $50K recognized)
• **Basis: $300,000**

**Deferred gain reduces basis!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Timing
sections.content:
**Replacement period timing:**

**Starts:** End of tax year in which gain realized
**Ends:** 2 years later (or 3 for condemned RE)

**NOT from date of conversion!**

**Example:**
• Casualty: March 2024
• Tax year ends: December 31, 2024
• Deadline: December 31, 2026
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Condemnation Blues? I've Been There!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, 'involuntary conversion' sounds like some sci-fi body snatchers thing, not taxes! But seriously, condemnations and casualties happen. I had a client whose warehouse burned down. Insurance paid out, but they were so stressed they almost forgot about the basis! Always remember: the goal is to defer the gain if possible. If they reinvest less than the insurance payout, that's when the taxman comes knocking. Keep calm, calculate the gain, and help them reinvest wisely!

sections.title: Forgetting the 'Not Reinvested' Magic Number
sections.content: A common slip-up is focusing solely on the 'gain realized' and forgetting to calculate the 'amount NOT reinvested.' Students often think the entire gain realized is taxable, which is incorrect. The taxable gain is the *lesser* of the gain realized OR the amount not reinvested in similar property. So, if someone realizes a $100,000 gain but reinvests $90,000, only $10,000 is taxable. Don't leave money on the table by overreporting income!

sections.title: Sarah's Stormy Situation
sections.content: Sarah's beachfront property was severely damaged by a hurricane. Her insurance company paid her $750,000, while her adjusted basis was $400,000. She's considering building a new, slightly smaller home for $600,000. The key takeaway here is that Sarah will only recognize a gain of $150,000 ($750,000 - $600,000), even though her realized gain is $350,000. By reinvesting a substantial portion of the insurance proceeds, she's successfully deferred a significant portion of the tax liability.

sections.title: Involuntary Conversions: Everyday Reality
sections.content: You'll encounter involuntary conversions more often than you think. Eminent domain cases are surprisingly common, especially with infrastructure projects. Clients may receive condemnation awards from the government for portions of their land. Understanding Section 1033 allows you to advise them on how to minimize their tax burden by reinvesting in similar property. Knowing the rules for replacement property and the time limits for reinvestment is crucial to providing sound tax planning advice.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Involuntary: Casualty, theft, condemnation
Gain deferred if reinvest in similar property
Replacement period: 2 years (3 for condemned RE)
Period starts at END of tax year of gain
Gain recognized = Lesser of gain or amount not reinvested
Basis of replacement reduced by deferred gain
§1033 requires similar use (narrower than §1031)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following events qualifies as an involuntary conversion under Section 1033 of the Internal Revenue Code?
options: A property owner sells land to a developer because they believe the developer will eventually condemn the land if they refuse to sell.
A business owner sells their warehouse after a fire destroyed a significant portion of it.
An individual sells their vacation home for a profit after receiving an unsolicited offer.
A corporation sells stock it owns in another corporation after the stock price declines significantly.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is A. A sale under threat of condemnation qualifies as an involuntary conversion. Option B qualifies, but the question asks about the event itself. The sale is the event, not the fire. Options C and D do not meet the definition of an involuntary conversion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the element of compulsion in an involuntary conversion.

knowledgeCheck.question: Barry's building with an adjusted basis of $400,000 was destroyed by a tornado. He received $600,000 in insurance proceeds. Barry then purchased a replacement building for $500,000. What is the amount of gain Barry must recognize?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $100,000. Barry realized a gain of $200,000 ($600,000 - $400,000). However, he only recognizes the portion of the gain that was not reinvested. He reinvested $500,000, leaving $100,000 not reinvested ($600,000 - $500,000). Therefore, his recognized gain is $100,000 (the lesser of the gain realized and the amount not reinvested).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Gain Recognized = Lesser of (Gain Realized, Amount Not Reinvested).

reveal.question: Define 'involuntary conversion' for tax purposes.
reveal.answer: An involuntary conversion occurs when property is compulsorily or involuntarily converted due to casualty (fire, storm, flood), theft, condemnation (eminent domain), or threat of condemnation, and converted into money (insurance, award) or other property.

title: Home Sale Exclusion (Section 121)
description: Master the $250K/$500K gain exclusion for principal residence

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The home sale exclusion is a HUGE tax benefit! Understanding the ownership/use tests and partial exclusion rules helps maximize this benefit. Up to $500K of gain can be excluded!

sections.title: Basic Exclusion
rows: At least one meets O + U

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 2-Out-of-5 Rule
sections.content:
**"Owned AND used 2 of last 5"**

**OWNERSHIP test:** Owned 2+ years
**USE test:** Lived there 2+ years

**During 5-year period ending on sale date**

**Don't have to be consecutive!**

sections.title: Ownership and Use Tests
sections.content:
**Both tests required:**

**Ownership:** Owned for 2+ years during 5-year period

**Use:** Used as principal residence for 2+ years during 5-year period

**Periods don't have to overlap!**

**Can count tacked period from spouse in divorce**

sections.title: Partial Exclusion
sections.content:
**When tests not fully met:**

**If sale due to:**
• Change in employment (50+ miles)
• Health reasons
• Unforeseen circumstances

**Formula:**
Partial exclusion = Max exclusion × (Months used / 24)

**Example:** Lived 18 months, single
$250,000 × 18/24 = **$187,500** exclusion

sections.title: Frequency Limitation
sections.content:
**One exclusion per 2 years:**

• Can't have excluded gain on another home in 2 years before sale
• Applies to each spouse individually

**Married couple:**
• If one spouse used exclusion within 2 years
• Maximum is $250,000 (other spouse's portion)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Depreciation Recapture
sections.content:
**If home used for business/rental:**

• Depreciation taken = Recaptured as ordinary income
• CANNOT exclude depreciation recapture
• Only gain above depreciation eligible

**Example:**
• Gain: $300,000
• Depreciation: $40,000
• Recaptured as ordinary: $40,000
• Excluded: $260,000 (if single)
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The Home Sale Exclusion: A Taxpayer's Best Friend
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. The Home Sale Exclusion is HUGE for your clients. I've had clients practically hug me when I tell them they don't owe taxes on a massive home sale profit. But don't get cocky! Remember the 2-out-of-5 year rule for both ownership AND use. People mess that up ALL the time. And DOCUMENT EVERYTHING. Trust me, the IRS loves to poke around on this one. Nail this, and you'll be a tax hero.

sections.title: Confusing Ownership and Use Requirements
sections.content: Students often think the ownership and use periods have to be consecutive or even overlapping. WRONG! You just need to have owned the home for 2 years within the 5-year window BEFORE the sale, AND used it as your principal residence for 2 years within that same 5-year window. They don't have to be the same 2 years. This is crucial, especially when dealing with rentals or time spent living elsewhere. Don't let this simple point sink your score!

sections.title: Sarah's Unexpected Move
sections.content: Sarah, a single taxpayer, lived in her home for 15 months before having to relocate for a new job 60 miles away. She sold her home for a $100,000 gain. Because of the job relocation, she qualifies for a partial exclusion. Her exclusion is $250,000 * (15/24) = $156,250. Therefore, she doesn't owe any tax on the gain! Remembering the partial exclusion formula can be a lifesaver for clients like Sarah.

sections.title: Home Sale Exclusion in Real Life
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter the Home Sale Exclusion constantly, especially during busy tax season. Clients often assume any profit from a home sale is taxable, so proactively asking about ownership history and reasons for selling is key. Knowing about the unforeseen circumstances exception and the partial exclusion calculation will differentiate you from less experienced preparers. This is a bread-and-butter tax planning opportunity, so master it now!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Exclusion: $250K single, $500K MFJ
2-out-of-5 rule: Owned AND used 2+ years
Periods don't have to be consecutive or overlap
Partial exclusion: For employment, health, unforeseen
Once every 2 years limitation
Depreciation cannot be excluded
Both spouses must meet tests for $500K

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Ownership and Use Test Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Mark and Sarah divorce on January 1, Year 5. As part of the divorce settlement, Sarah receives the house they owned jointly and used as their principal residence since January 1, Year 1. Sarah sells the house on March 31, Year 6. Assuming Sarah is single at the time of the sale and no other exclusions have been taken, what is the maximum exclusion Sarah can claim from the sale of the house?
options: The exclusion is not available due to the sale occurring within one year of the divorce.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah meets both the ownership and use tests. She can tack on Mark's ownership period (Year 1-Year 5) to her own (Year 5-Year 6). This means she owned the house for more than two years within the five-year period ending on the date of sale. She also used the house as her principal residence for more than two years within the same five-year period. Therefore, she can exclude up to $250,000 of the gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider the tacking rule for ownership in divorce situations.

reveal.question: List the three reasons that can allow for a partial home sale exclusion when the ownership and use tests are not fully met.
reveal.answer:
1. Change in employment (50+ miles)
2. Health reasons
3. Unforeseen circumstances

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a single taxpayer, was unexpectedly transferred by his employer to a new location 600 miles away. He owned and lived in his home as his principal residence for 15 months before selling it. What is the maximum amount of gain John can exclude from the sale of his home?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because the sale was due to a change in employment more than 50 miles away, John is eligible for a partial exclusion. The calculation is $250,000 (maximum exclusion for single) * (15 months / 24 months) = $156,250.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to apply the partial exclusion formula when the ownership and use tests are not fully met due to a qualifying reason.

title: Capital Gains and Losses
description: Master capital gain rates and loss limitation rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Capital gains have preferential tax rates! Understanding the netting process, rate brackets, and loss limitations is essential for investment tax planning. This is heavily tested on REG!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Holding Period
sections.content:
**">1 Year = Long-term"**

**Count from day AFTER acquisition**
**To day OF disposition**

**Example:**
• Buy: January 15, 2024
• Must sell AFTER: January 15, 2025
• January 15, 2025 = Still short-term!
• January 16, 2025 = Long-term

sections.title: Netting Process
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Net within each category
• ST gains vs ST losses
• LT gains vs LT losses

**Step 2:** Net across categories
• Net ST against Net LT

**Results:**
• Net LTCG: Preferential rate
• Net STCG: Ordinary rate
• Net loss: $3,000 deduction

sections.title: Capital Loss Rules
sections.content:
**Individuals:**
• Net capital loss: Deduct up to $3,000/year
• Excess carries forward indefinitely
• Character (ST/LT) preserved in carryforward

**Corporations:**
• No deduction against ordinary income
• Carryback 3 years, forward 5 years
• Only offsets capital gains

sections.title: Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)
sections.content:
**3.8% surtax on investment income:**

**Applies when MAGI exceeds:**
• $200,000 (single)
• $250,000 (MFJ)

**Investment income includes:**
• Interest, dividends
• Capital gains
• Rental income
• Passive income

**Top effective rate: 20% + 3.8% = 23.8%**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Wash Sale Rule
sections.content:
**Loss disallowed if:**

• Sell at loss AND
• Buy substantially identical within 30 days before/after

**61-day window total!**

**Effect:**
• Loss is deferred, not lost
• Added to basis of new shares

**Does NOT apply to gains!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Capital Gains: The Tax Pro's Secret Weapon (Sort Of)
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be honest. Capital gains and losses can be a headache. I've seen seasoned pros stumble over netting rules. But here's the deal: get this right, and you’ll save your clients (and yourself) tons of tax. Think of it like this: ST is like regular income, LT gets the VIP treatment with lower rates. Don't mix them up! And for the love of all that is holy, remember the $3,000 loss limit for individuals. It's the tax code's little way of saying, 'Hey, even Uncle Sam feels your pain... a little.'

sections.title: The $3,000 Deduction Trap!
sections.content: Students often forget that the $3,000 capital loss deduction for individuals is an *annual* limit. They'll see a $10,000 net capital loss and deduct the whole thing. Nope! You can only deduct $3,000 against ordinary income each year. The remaining $7,000 carries forward indefinitely, retaining its character (short-term or long-term). Always double-check the prior year's carryforward schedule to make sure you're not missing a deduction. This is a HUGE point on the exam and in real-world practice.

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Snafu: The Importance of Netting
sections.content: Sarah sold stock this year. She had a $5,000 short-term capital gain from one stock and a $8,000 long-term capital loss from another. Many preparers might rush to offset the $8,000 loss against ordinary income up to the $3,000 limit, but that’s wrong. First, she needs to net the $5,000 ST gain against the $8,000 LT loss, resulting in a net LT capital loss of $3,000. She can then use the entire $3,000 loss to offset ordinary income. Netting is crucial!

sections.title: Capital Gains: Your Daily Dose of Tax Reality
sections.content: As a CPA, you'll encounter capital gains and losses constantly. Think about preparing individual tax returns for clients who sell stock, real estate, or even cryptocurrency. Understanding the netting rules and loss limitations is essential for accurate tax planning and compliance. You'll also need to explain these rules clearly to your clients, helping them understand the tax implications of their investment decisions. It's not just about numbers; it's about providing valuable advice.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Long-term: >1 year; Preferential rates 0%/15%/20%
Short-term: ≤1 year; Ordinary rates
Collectibles max: 28%; Unrecaptured §1250: 25%
Net losses: $3,000/year deduction (individuals)
Carryforward: Indefinite, character preserved
NIIT: 3.8% on investment income above thresholds
Wash sale: 30-day rule defers loss

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An individual taxpayer has the following capital gains and losses for the current year: Short-term capital gain of $5,000, Short-term capital loss of $8,000, Long-term capital gain of $12,000, Long-term capital loss of $2,000. What is the net capital gain or loss that will be included in the individual's adjusted gross income (AGI)?
options: Net long-term capital gain of $7,000
Net long-term capital gain of $9,000
Net short-term capital loss of $3,000
Net long-term capital gain of $3,000
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, net within each category: ST gain of $5,000 - ST loss of $8,000 = Net ST loss of $3,000. LT gain of $12,000 - LT loss of $2,000 = Net LT gain of $10,000. Next, net across categories: Net LT gain of $10,000 - Net ST loss of $3,000 = Net LT gain of $7,000. Since there is still a net long-term capital gain, it is taxed at preferential rates.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to net within categories (ST and LT) before netting across categories.

reveal.question: What is the maximum amount of net capital loss that an individual can deduct against ordinary income in a single tax year?
reveal.answer: An individual can deduct up to $3,000 of net capital loss against ordinary income in a single tax year. Any excess capital loss can be carried forward indefinitely.

rows.label: Deduction Against Ordinary Income
values: Up to $3,000 per year
Not allowed

rows.label: Carryforward
values: Indefinitely
5 years

rows.label: Carryback
values: Not allowed
3 years

rows.label: Use of Carryback/Carryforward
values: N/A
Only offsets capital gains

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation has a net capital loss of $20,000 in 2024. In which of the following years can XYZ Corporation use this capital loss to offset capital gains?
options: Only in 2029
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Corporations can carry back capital losses 3 years and forward 5 years. Thus, the loss from 2024 can be carried back to 2021, 2022 and 2023 and carried forward to 2025, 2026, 2027, and 2028.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the carryback and carryforward periods for corporate capital losses.

title: Professional Responsibility: Circular 230
description: Understand IRS rules governing tax practitioners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Circular 230 governs practice before the IRS! Understanding practitioner duties, standards for positions, and penalties is essential for professional responsibility questions on REG!

sections.title: Who Is Covered?
sections.content:
**Practitioners authorized to practice before IRS:**

• Attorneys
• CPAs
• Enrolled agents
• Enrolled actuaries
• Enrolled retirement plan agents

**"Practice before IRS" includes:**
Preparing returns, representation, correspondence

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Position Standards
sections.content:
**Standards (Low → High):**

**Not frivolous:** ~10% (barely colorable)
**Reasonable basis:** ~20% (disclosed positions)
**Substantial authority:** ~40% (undisclosed, no penalty)
**More likely than not:** >50% (tax shelters)

**Higher standard = Stronger position needed**

sections.title: Due Diligence
sections.content:
**Practitioner must:**

• Exercise due diligence in preparing/filing
• Not rely on client info if appears incorrect
• Make reasonable inquiries
• Advise client of consequences of positions

**Cannot just accept what client says!**

sections.title: Key Prohibitions
sections.content:
**May NOT:**

• Charge unconscionable fees
• Endorse or negotiate client's IRS check
• Practice during suspension/disbarment
• Assist disbarred practitioners
• Give false or misleading information
• Knowingly submit false documents

**May NOT represent conflicting interests** (without consent)

sections.title: Written Advice Rules
sections.content:
**When providing written tax advice:**

• Base on reasonable factual assumptions
• Consider all relevant facts
• Don't rely on unreasonable assumptions
• Don't take into account likelihood of audit

**Must be based on good faith analysis**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Penalties
sections.content:
**OPR (Office of Professional Responsibility) can:**

• Censure (public reprimand)
• Suspend (temporary)
• Disbar (permanent)
• Impose monetary penalty

**Violations include:**
• Willful violations
• Reckless conduct
• Gross incompetence
• Pattern of incompetence
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Client Said WHAT?! (Due Diligence, Folks!)
sections.content: Okay, listen up. I've prepped hundreds of returns and the *craziest* things come out of clients' mouths. Like, 'I'm *pretty sure* I donated my yacht to charity...' or 'Yeah, I expensed that trip to Vegas as a business seminar.' Circular 230 is your shield! Don't just blindly accept their story. Dig a little. Ask for documentation. Protect yourself (and them, honestly) from potential audit nightmares. Trust, but verify. If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is. This isn't about being nosy, it's about doing your job ethically and competently.

sections.title: Assuming Client Honesty = Professional Responsibility?
sections.content: A frequent error students make is thinking that if a client *seems* honest, they're off the hook for further inquiry. Circular 230 explicitly states you can't simply rely on client-provided information if it appears incorrect, inconsistent, or incomplete. The key here is 'appears'. Even a seemingly honest client can be mistaken or misinformed. For example, a client might sincerely believe they qualify for a certain deduction, but without proper documentation or a clear understanding of the rules, their belief doesn't make it so. You MUST make reasonable inquiries and advise them of the potential consequences of taking an unsupported position.

sections.title: Sarah's Sketchy Schedule C
sections.content: Sarah, a new CPA, is reviewing a Schedule C for a client. The client claims $25,000 in 'marketing expenses' with no supporting documentation. Sarah, wanting to please her client, files the return as is. Big mistake! Circular 230 requires Sarah to exercise due diligence. Even without blatant dishonesty, the lack of documentation and high expense amount should trigger further inquiry. Sarah needed to ask for receipts, invoices, or other proof before filing the return, or advise the client of the potential penalties for taking an unsubstantiated deduction.

sections.title: Circular 230: Your Daily Defense
sections.content: Every single tax season, you'll encounter situations where Circular 230 principles are critical. It might be a client trying to stretch the definition of 'business expense' or claiming deductions they aren't entitled to. For example, you might have a client with a home office deduction that seems disproportionately large compared to their income. That's your cue to investigate further. Knowing Circular 230 isn't just about passing the REG exam; it's about protecting yourself and your firm from IRS scrutiny, maintaining your professional reputation, and providing ethical tax advice to your clients day in and day out.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Circular 230 governs IRS practice
Applies to: CPAs, attorneys, enrolled agents
Signing returns: Reasonable basis required
Due diligence: Cannot blindly accept client info
Conflicts: Written consent required
Cannot negotiate client's IRS refund check
Penalties: Censure, suspension, disbarment

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following individuals is NOT considered a 'practitioner' authorized to practice before the IRS under Circular 230?
options: A licensed attorney
A Certified Public Accountant (CPA)
An Enrolled Agent
A tax preparer who only prepares individual income tax returns and is not a CPA, attorney, or enrolled agent
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Circular 230 specifically defines practitioners as attorneys, CPAs, enrolled agents, enrolled actuaries, and enrolled retirement plan agents authorized to practice before the IRS. A tax preparer who only prepares individual income tax returns is not automatically considered a practitioner unless they fall into one of those categories.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the specific list of individuals authorized to practice before the IRS according to Circular 230.

reveal.question: What is a practitioner's responsibility regarding client information that appears incorrect?
reveal.answer: A practitioner must not rely on client information if it appears incorrect. They must make reasonable inquiries to verify the information and advise the client of the potential consequences if the information is indeed incorrect.

knowledgeCheck.question: A CPA is preparing a client's tax return. The client provides documentation showing a large charitable contribution, but the amount seems unusually high compared to the client's income. The CPA asks the client about it, and the client assures them the donation was legitimate and refuses to provide further documentation. According to Circular 230, what is the CPA's best course of action?
options: File the return as is, relying on the client's assurance.
Disclose the potential issue on the tax return, but still claim the deduction.
Refuse to sign the return and withdraw from the engagement.
Make further reasonable inquiries and gather additional documentation to substantiate the deduction before filing the return.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Circular 230 requires practitioners to exercise due diligence. Simply relying on the client's assurance is not sufficient. The CPA must make further reasonable inquiries to verify the legitimacy of the deduction before filing the return. If the client refuses to provide sufficient documentation, the CPA may need to consider withdrawing from the engagement, but further inquiry is the first step.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the CPA's responsibility to verify information and not blindly accept client statements.

title: Agency Law
description: Understand principal-agent relationships and liability

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Agency law determines when one person can bind another! Understanding types of authority, agent duties, and principal liability is essential for business law questions on REG!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Authority Types
sections.content:
**"EIAR"**

**E**xpress - "You may do X"
**I**mplied - Reasonable to accomplish X
**A**pparent - Looks like authority to 3rd party
**R**atification - "I approve what you did"

**All can bind principal!**

sections.title: Agent Duties to Principal
sections.content:
**Fiduciary duties:**

• **Loyalty:** Act in principal's interest
• **Obedience:** Follow instructions
• **Care:** Use reasonable skill
• **Disclosure:** Report material information
• **Accounting:** Account for property/money

**Agent must put principal's interests first!**

sections.title: Principal Duties to Agent
sections.content:
**Principal must:**

• Pay agreed compensation
• Reimburse expenses
• Indemnify for losses from authorized acts
• Cooperate in agent's performance

**Can be modified by agreement**

sections.title: Liability to Third Parties
sections.content:
**Principal's liability:**
• Bound by authorized acts
• Bound by apparent authority
• Liable for agent's torts in scope

**Agent's liability:**
• Personally liable if principal undisclosed
• Personally liable if exceeds authority
• Not liable if disclosed and authorized

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Disclosed vs Undisclosed
sections.content:
**Disclosed principal:** Third party knows identity
→ Principal bound, Agent usually not

**Partially disclosed:** Knows agency, not identity
→ Both may be liable

**Undisclosed:** Third party doesn't know
→ Agent personally liable
→ Principal also liable when discovered
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Agency Law: It's All About the Relationships
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's talk agency. Think of it like this: you're hiring someone to be *you* in a specific context. Mess this up, and it's like accidentally granting someone power of attorney to sell your house when you just wanted them to pick up your dry cleaning. Seriously, understand the fiduciary duties. Loyalty, obedience, care – it’s the whole package. And remember, it's a two-way street. If you're the principal, treat your agent right! Pay them, cover their costs, and don't hang them out to dry when they act on your behalf.

sections.title: Mixing Up Indemnification and Reimbursement
sections.content: A frequent error is confusing indemnification with reimbursement. Reimbursement covers expenses an agent incurs while *performing* authorized acts (like travel costs for a business trip). Indemnification protects the agent from *losses* arising from those authorized acts (like a lawsuit resulting from following the principal's instructions, even if those instructions were flawed). The key difference? Reimbursement is for expenses, indemnification is for liabilities or damages. Don't let a $500 reimbursement turn into a $50,000 legal battle because you didn't understand indemnification!

sections.title: Sarah's Slippery Slope: Undisclosed Conflict
sections.content: Sarah, an agent representing a real estate investor, also has a secret ownership stake in a property her principal is considering buying. She doesn't disclose this conflict of interest. This is a clear breach of her fiduciary duty of loyalty. Sarah is putting her own interests ahead of her principal's, potentially influencing the transaction for personal gain. Even if she believes the property is a good deal, her lack of disclosure creates a serious ethical and legal problem.

sections.title: Power of Attorney: Agency Law in Action
sections.content: You'll see agency law principles constantly when reviewing Powers of Attorney (POAs). These documents explicitly grant someone the authority to act on another's behalf. As a CPA, you might review a POA to determine if someone is authorized to sign tax returns or manage financial assets. Always verify the scope of the agent's authority and whether the POA is still valid. A poorly drafted or outdated POA can cause major headaches for clients and potentially expose you to liability for relying on it incorrectly.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Authority: Express, Implied, Apparent, Ratification
Agent owes fiduciary duties to principal
Principal liable for authorized agent acts
Apparent authority binds principal to third parties
Agent personally liable if principal undisclosed
Ratification requires knowledge and capacity
Principal liable for agent torts in scope of employment

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Agent's Fiduciary Duty: Test Your Knowledge

knowledgeCheck.question: An agent, acting within the scope of their authority, unknowingly enters into a contract that results in a loss for the principal. The agent acted with reasonable skill and care, but failed to disclose a minor potential conflict of interest that, in hindsight, contributed to the loss. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the agent's liability?
options: The agent is liable for the loss because they have a strict fiduciary duty to prevent any loss to the principal.
The agent is liable for the loss because they failed to disclose the potential conflict of interest, violating their duty of loyalty.
The agent is not liable for the loss because they acted with reasonable skill and care and the conflict of interest was minor and unknown.
The agent is liable for the loss because an agent is always liable for losses incurred by the principal as a result of the agent's actions.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The agent's duty of care requires reasonable skill. Since they acted with reasonable skill and care, and the undisclosed conflict was minor and didn't significantly contribute, they are likely not liable. The duty of loyalty requires disclosure of material information, which this was not.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the materiality of the undisclosed information and the agent's level of care.

reveal.question: List the four primary duties a principal owes to their agent.
reveal.answer:
The four primary duties a principal owes to their agent are: 

1.  Pay agreed compensation
2.  Reimburse expenses
3.  Indemnify for losses from authorized acts
4.  Cooperate in agent's performance

rows.label: Primary Focus
values: Acting in the principal's best interest
Facilitating the agent's ability to act on their behalf

rows.label: Key Responsibilities
values: Loyalty, Obedience, Care, Disclosure, Accounting
Compensation, Reimbursement, Indemnification, Cooperation

rows.label: Breach Consequences
values: Liability for losses, Termination of agency
Agent can sue for breach of contract, Termination of agency

rows.label: Flexibility
values: Fiduciary duties are generally strict, but details can be modified
Duties can be modified by agreement between the parties

title: Tax-Free Tip Income (H.R.1)
description: Understand the new tax exemption for qualifying tip income

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: H.R.1 (One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025) introduced a new above-the-line deduction for qualifying tip income! This is a major change affecting millions of hospitality workers and their tax planning.

sections.title: Key Provisions
rows: Applies to tips received after enactment
Tips must be reported on W-2

sections.title: Qualifying Tips
sections.content:
**Tips that qualify for the deduction:**

• Cash tips received directly from customers
• Credit card tips processed through employer
• Tips from tip pools and sharing arrangements
• Service charges designated as tips

**Must be properly reported to employer!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Tip Deduction Rules
sections.content:
**"TIPS"** for qualification:

**T**ipped occupation (defined categories)
**I**ncome under threshold (phase-out applies)
**P**roperly reported (on W-2)
**S**ervice-related (food, hospitality, beauty)

**Above-the-line = Available even without itemizing!**

sections.title: Income Phase-Outs
sections.content:
**Deduction phases out for higher earners:**

• Single filers: Phase-out begins at $160,000 AGI
• MFJ filers: Phase-out begins at $320,000 AGI
• Completely phased out $40,000 above threshold

**Calculate modified AGI excluding tips for threshold**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Still Subject to FICA
sections.content:
**Tips are exempt from INCOME tax, NOT payroll tax!**

• Social Security tax still applies
• Medicare tax still applies
• Employer FICA obligation unchanged

**Only the federal income tax exclusion is new!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: The Tip Tax Tango: Don't Get Stepped On!
sections.content: Alright, folks, let's be real. Tip income can feel like free money, but the IRS wants its cut! This new tax-free tip income provision is a game changer, but only if you play by the rules. I've seen too many servers think 'cash is king' and conveniently forget to report a portion of their tips. Trust me, the IRS is getting smarter with data matching, and unreported tips are a red flag. So, understand these rules, advise your clients well, and avoid the audit tango!

sections.title: AGI Ambush: Where Students Stumble
sections.content: A frequent mistake is using regular AGI instead of modified AGI when calculating the phase-out. Remember, for this deduction, you calculate modified AGI *excluding* the tip income. Students often overlook this crucial detail and incorrectly determine eligibility. The key is to recalculate AGI, subtracting the qualifying tip income. If the recalculated AGI is above the phase-out threshold, then you must account for the deduction phase-out. Always double-check which AGI is required for each deduction!

sections.title: Sarah's Server Struggle: Reporting Right
sections.content: Sarah works as a server and receives $10,000 in qualifying tips, properly reported to her employer. Her AGI, *excluding* the tip income, is $175,000. Because Sarah is single, the deduction is phased out. If her modified AGI was below $160,000, she could have deducted the full amount. Understanding the phase-out rules saved Sarah from overpaying her taxes this year, and from potential penalties in the future.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Client Counseling
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter this most often when preparing tax returns for clients in the service industry. Servers, bartenders, delivery drivers – anyone who receives tips. Educate them about the importance of proper tip reporting and the potential benefits of this new deduction. Providing proactive advice regarding the AGI thresholds can significantly reduce their tax liability and demonstrate your value as a trusted advisor. Be sure to ask the right questions about their income sources!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: H.R.1 creates above-the-line deduction for qualifying tips
Applies to food service, hospitality, beauty workers
Tips must be properly reported to employer
Phase-outs apply for higher income taxpayers
FICA taxes still apply - only income tax exempted
Effective for tax years beginning after 2024

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Jane, a waitress, receives the following during the year: $10,000 in cash tips directly from customers, $5,000 in tips added to credit card payments, and $2,000 as her share of a mandatory service charge distributed by the restaurant to all servers. Jane properly reports all tips to her employer. Assuming she meets all other requirements, what amount of these tips qualifies for the potential tax-free tip income deduction?
options: None of the tips qualify.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: All of the listed tip income qualifies for the deduction. Cash tips and credit card tips received directly from customers qualify, as well as tips from tip pools (the mandatory service charge distributed to servers is considered part of a tip pool).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider all the types of tips listed in the lesson.

reveal.question: What is the Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) threshold at which the tax-free tip income deduction begins to phase out for a taxpayer filing as Married Filing Jointly (MFJ)?
reveal.answer: The deduction begins to phase out for MFJ filers with an AGI of $320,000.

knowledgeCheck.question: Mark, a single filer, has an AGI of $170,000, which includes $5,000 in qualifying tip income. To determine if he is eligible for the tax-free tip income deduction, how should Mark calculate his modified AGI?
options: Subtract the $5,000 in tip income from his AGI of $170,000.
Add the $5,000 in tip income to his AGI of $170,000.
Multiply his AGI of $170,000 by 5,000.
His AGI is already correctly stated, no modification is needed.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: To determine if the AGI limitation applies, modified AGI must be calculated. This is done by subtracting the tip income from the AGI. In this case, Mark's modified AGI is $165,000 ($170,000 - $5,000).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall how the modified AGI is calculated for this deduction.

title: SALT Deduction Changes (H.R.1)
description: Navigate the revised State and Local Tax deduction limits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The $10,000 SALT cap has been one of the most debated provisions! H.R.1 modifies this limitation. Understanding the current rules and changes is essential for REG!

sections.title: What Qualifies as SALT
sections.content:
**Deductible state and local taxes:**

• State and local income taxes, OR
• State and local sales taxes (elect one)
• Real property taxes
• Personal property taxes

**NOT included:**
• Foreign taxes (separate FTC)
• Fees and assessments (non-tax)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SALT Components
sections.content:
**"SIPR"** for SALT:

**S**tate income tax (or sales tax)
**I**s limited to the cap
**P**roperty tax (real property)
**R**eal and personal property taxes

**Choose income OR sales, not both!**

sections.title: Phase-Out Structure
sections.content:
**Higher cap phases out for wealthy:**

• Full $40,000 cap: AGI under $500,000 (MFJ)
• Reduced cap: AGI $500,000 - $1,000,000
• Minimum cap: AGI over $1,000,000

**Single filers: Half of MFJ thresholds**

sections.title: Married Filing Separately
sections.content:
**Special rules for MFS:**

• Cap is HALF of the MFJ amount
• $20,000 maximum (under new rules)
• Phase-out thresholds also halved

**Marriage penalty consideration!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Workarounds
sections.content:
**Common SALT workarounds:**

• Pass-through entity tax elections (some states)
• Charitable contributions to state credit programs
• Business deductions on Schedule C/E

**IRS has challenged some aggressive strategies!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: SALT Deduction: The Government Giveth (a Little)
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest, the $10,000 SALT cap? It's a rounding error for some folks, especially in high-tax states. But hey, it's something, right? I've seen clients get so hung up on itemizing every single deduction that they miss the forest for the trees. Don't be that person. Focus on the big picture. Remember, it's the *lesser* of the actual amount or the $10,000 cap. And for the love of all that is holy, pick income or sales tax wisely! Do the math!

sections.title: SALT Cap Confusion: MFJ vs. Single Filers
sections.content: A very common mistake is applying the Married Filing Jointly (MFJ) AGI thresholds to Single filers. Students forget that the AGI thresholds for phasing out the higher SALT caps are HALF of the MFJ amounts for single filers. So, a single filer with an AGI over $500,000 starts to see a reduction in their potential higher SALT cap. Always double-check filing status and adjust the thresholds accordingly. It's an easy point to lose on the exam if you're not careful!

sections.title: The Case of Bob's Property Taxes
sections.content: Bob, a CPA candidate, lives in New Jersey and owns a home. His real property taxes are $12,000, and his state income tax is $5,000. Bob, eager to maximize his deductions, initially tried to deduct the full $12,000 in property taxes. However, he quickly realized that due to the $10,000 SALT cap, he could only deduct $10,000 in total, even though his combined state and local taxes exceeded that amount. This is a prime example of how the SALT cap limits deductions, regardless of the individual components.

sections.title: SALT in the Real World: Client Communication
sections.content: In practice, you'll be explaining the SALT deduction limitations to clients constantly. Many clients still believe they can deduct all their state and local taxes. You need to be able to clearly explain the $10,000 cap and how it impacts their tax liability. Prepare talking points that address common misconceptions and be ready to show them the math. This is especially important for clients in high-tax states like California, New York, and New Jersey.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SALT = State income/sales + Property taxes
Original TCJA cap: $10,000 ($5,000 MFS)
H.R.1 increases cap with phase-outs for high earners
Must choose between income tax OR sales tax
Property taxes are always included in SALT
Business taxes on Sch C/E are NOT subject to SALT cap

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following taxes or fees is NOT deductible as part of the State and Local Tax (SALT) deduction for an individual?
options: Real property taxes paid on a primary residence.
State income taxes withheld from wages.
A city assessment for a new sidewalk benefiting the taxpayer's property.
Personal property taxes on a vehicle.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: City assessments for improvements that benefit a specific property are considered fees, not taxes, and are therefore not deductible as part of the SALT deduction.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the difference between a tax and a fee for a specific service or improvement.

reveal.question: What is the Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) threshold above which the SALT deduction cap begins to be reduced for a married couple filing jointly?
reveal.answer: The AGI threshold is $500,000 for married couples filing jointly. Above this amount, the full $40,000 SALT deduction cap is no longer available.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, a single filer, has an AGI of $600,000. Her state income taxes are $8,000, real property taxes are $7,000, and personal property taxes are $500. What is the maximum amount of SALT Sarah can deduct?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum SALT deduction is capped at $10,000 regardless of the total amount of state and local taxes paid. Therefore, even though Sarah's total SALT would be $15,500 ($8,000 + $7,000 + $500), she can only deduct $10,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the SALT deduction limit for single filers.

title: Enhanced Child Tax Credit (H.R.1)
description: Apply the expanded Child Tax Credit provisions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The Child Tax Credit is one of the most valuable family tax benefits! H.R.1 enhances the credit amount and modifies eligibility. Understanding these changes is critical for REG!

sections.title: Qualifying Child Requirements
sections.content:
**Child must meet ALL tests:**

• **Age:** Under 18 at year-end (increased from 17)
• **Relationship:** Son, daughter, stepchild, etc.
• **Residency:** Lived with taxpayer > 6 months
• **Support:** Child didn't provide > 50% of own support
• **SSN:** Must have valid SSN
• **Dependent:** Claimed as dependent on return

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CTC Tests
sections.content:
**"ARRSSD"** for qualifying:

**A**ge - Under 18 (H.R.1 change!)
**R**elationship - Close family member
**R**esidency - Lived together > 6 months
**S**upport - Child not self-supporting
**S**SN - Valid Social Security Number
**D**ependent - Claimed on return

**Remember: 18 is the NEW age limit!**

sections.title: Refundable vs. Nonrefundable
sections.content:
**Two components of CTC:**

**Nonrefundable:** Reduces tax to zero
**Refundable (ACTC):** Up to $2,000 refundable

**ACTC calculation:**
• 15% of earned income over $2,500
• Capped at refundable limit

**Must have earned income for refundable portion!**

sections.title: Phase-Out Calculation
sections.content:
**Credit reduces for high income:**

• MFJ: Phase-out begins at $400,000
• Other: Phase-out begins at $200,000
• Reduction: $50 per $1,000 over threshold

**Example:** MFJ with $420,000 AGI
= $20,000 over ÷ $1,000 × $50
= $1,000 reduction per child

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SSN Requirement
sections.content:
**SSN is STRICTLY required for CTC!**

• ITIN holders do NOT qualify
• SSN must be issued BEFORE due date
• Adopted children: ATIN acceptable temporarily

**No SSN = No CTC (but may get $500 ODC)**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: CTC: The Under-18 Rule is a Gotcha!
sections.content: Alright, listen up! I've seen CPAs (and experienced ones, mind you!) trip over the Child Tax Credit age requirement. It's under 18 *at year-end*. Not 'turned 18 during the year.' So, if little Timmy turns 18 on December 31st, you're out of luck. No CTC for you! Don't assume just because they were 17 for most of the year. This little detail can cost your client (and you!) a penalty. Double-check those birthdates, people!

sections.title: Mixing Refundable and Nonrefundable CTC
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking the *entire* Child Tax Credit is refundable. It isn't! It's a two-tiered system. First, the nonrefundable portion reduces the tax liability down to zero. THEN, if any credit is left over *and* the taxpayer has earned income above the $2,500 threshold, they might qualify for the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC), which is the refundable part. Don't just assume every family gets $2,000 back per child. You need to understand the calculation flow!

sections.title: Sarah's Residency Woes
sections.content: Sarah, a single mom, has her son, Michael, living with her for 5 months and 2 weeks. Michael then spends the rest of the year with his father under a court-ordered custody arrangement. Because Michael didn't live with Sarah for more than 6 months, even though she provides over half his support, Sarah cannot claim the Child Tax Credit for Michael. The residency test is a strict requirement.

sections.title: CTC in Tax Prep Software
sections.content: In practice, your tax preparation software will usually guide you through the Child Tax Credit eligibility questions. However, it's crucial to understand the underlying rules. The software won't catch everything! For example, it might flag a missing SSN, but it won't automatically know if the child provided more than half of their own support. You need to ask the right questions and document your findings to ensure accuracy and compliance.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: H.R.1 increases CTC to $2,500 per qualifying child
Age limit increased to under 18 (was under 17)
Refundable portion (ACTC) increased to $2,000
Phase-out: $400,000 MFJ, $200,000 others
Valid SSN required - no ITIN
Must have earned income for refundable portion

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios would disqualify a child from being claimed for the Enhanced Child Tax Credit under H.R.1?
